**Belgium**,[[b]](#cite_note-15) officially the **Kingdom of Belgium**,[[c]](#cite_note-16) is a country in [Northwestern Europe](/wiki/Northwestern_Europe "Northwestern Europe"). Situated in a coastal lowland region known as the [Low Countries](/wiki/Low_Countries "Low Countries"), it is bordered by the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") to the north, [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") to the east, [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg "Luxembourg") to the southeast, [France](/wiki/France "France") to the south, and the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea") to the west. The country also shares a maritime boundary with the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") to the northwest. Belgium covers an area of 30,689 km2 (11,849 sq mi)[[5]](#cite_note-area-5) and has a population of more than 11.8 million;[[8]](#cite_note-population-8) its [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") of 383/km2 (990/sq mi) ranks [22nd in the world](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_population_density "List of countries and dependencies by population density") and [sixth in Europe](/wiki/Area_and_population_of_European_countries "Area and population of European countries"). The capital and [largest metropolitan region](/wiki/Metropolitan_areas_in_Belgium "Metropolitan areas in Belgium") is [Brussels](/wiki/City_of_Brussels "City of Brussels");[[d]](#cite_note-18) other major cities are [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp"), [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent"), [Charleroi](/wiki/Charleroi "Charleroi"), [Liège](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge "Liège"), [Bruges](/wiki/Bruges "Bruges"), [Namur](/wiki/Namur "Namur"), and [Leuven](/wiki/Leuven "Leuven").

Belgium is a [parliamentary constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Parliamentary_system "Parliamentary system") with a complex [federal](/wiki/Federation "Federation") system structured on regional and linguistic grounds. The country is divided into three highly autonomous [regions](/wiki/Communities,_regions_and_language_areas_of_Belgium "Communities, regions and language areas of Belgium"):[[15]](#cite_note-dBWtI-19) the [Flemish Region](/wiki/Flemish_Region "Flemish Region") (Flanders) in the north, the [Walloon Region](/wiki/Wallonia "Wallonia") (Wallonia) in the south, and the [Brussels-Capital Region](/wiki/Brussels-Capital_Region "Brussels-Capital Region") in the middle.[[16]](#cite_note-Belgian_Constitution-20) Belgium is also home to two main linguistic communities: the Dutch-speaking [Flemish Community](/wiki/Flemish_Community "Flemish Community"), which constitutes about 60 percent of the population, and the French-speaking [French Community](/wiki/French_Community_of_Belgium "French Community of Belgium"),[[e]](#cite_note-22) which constitutes about 40 percent of the population; a small [German-speaking Community](/wiki/German-speaking_Community_of_Belgium "German-speaking Community of Belgium"), comprising around one percent of the population, exists in the [East Cantons](/wiki/Eupen-Malmedy "Eupen-Malmedy"). Belgium's linguistic diversity and related political conflicts are reflected in its complex system of governance, made up of [six different governments](/wiki/List_of_governments_in_Belgium "List of governments in Belgium"). Belgium is a [developed country](/wiki/Developed_country "Developed country") with an advanced [high-income economy](/wiki/World_Bank_high-income_economy "World Bank high-income economy"). It is one of the [six founding members](/wiki/Inner_Six "Inner Six") of the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), with its capital of Brussels serving as the *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* capital of the EU, hosting the official seats of the [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission"), the [Council of the European Union](/wiki/Council_of_the_European_Union "Council of the European Union"), the [European Council](/wiki/European_Council "European Council"), and one of two seats of the [European Parliament](/wiki/European_Parliament "European Parliament") (the other being [Strasbourg](/wiki/Strasbourg "Strasbourg")). Brussels also hosts the headquarters of many major international organizations, such as [NATO](/wiki/NATO_headquarters "NATO headquarters").[[f]](#cite_note-23)

In [antiquity](/wiki/Ancient_history "Ancient history"), present-day Belgium was dominated by the [Belgae](/wiki/Belgae "Belgae") before being annexed into the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") in the mid first century BC. During the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), Belgium's central location kept it relatively prosperous and connected both commercially and politically to its larger neighbours; it was part of the [Carolingian Empire](/wiki/Carolingian_Empire "Carolingian Empire"), the succeeding [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire"), and subsequently the [Burgundian Netherlands](/wiki/Burgundian_Netherlands "Burgundian Netherlands"). Following rule by [Habsburg Spain](/wiki/Spanish_Netherlands "Spanish Netherlands") (1556–1714), the [Austrian Habsburgs](/wiki/Austrian_Netherlands "Austrian Netherlands") (1714–1794), and [Revolutionary France](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution") (1794–1815), most of modern-day Belgium was incorporated into the [United Kingdom of the Netherlands](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_the_Netherlands "United Kingdom of the Netherlands") after the [Congress of Vienna](/wiki/Congress_of_Vienna "Congress of Vienna") in 1815. Centuries of being contested and controlled by various European powers earned Belgium the moniker "the Battlefield of Europe",[[18]](#cite_note-Hass-24) a reputation reinforced in the 20th century by both [world wars](/wiki/World_war "World war").

An independent Belgium was established in 1830 following the [Belgian Revolution](/wiki/Belgian_Revolution "Belgian Revolution"). In the 19th century it was one of the earliest participants of the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"),[[19]](#cite_note-Fitzmaurice-25)[[20]](#cite_note-EUbusiness-26) and the first country in [continental Europe](/wiki/Continental_Europe "Continental Europe") to become industrialised.[[21]](#cite_note-27) By the early 20th century, it possessed [several colonies](/wiki/Belgian_colonial_empire "Belgian colonial empire"), notably the [Belgian Congo](/wiki/Belgian_Congo "Belgian Congo") and [Ruanda-Urundi](/wiki/Ruanda-Urundi "Ruanda-Urundi"),[[22]](#cite_note-Karl-28)[[g]](#cite_note-30) which gained independence between 1960 and 1962.[[24]](#cite_note-31) The second half of the 20th century was marked by rising tensions between the Dutch-speakers and French-speakers, fueled by differences in political culture and the unequal economic development of Flanders and Wallonia. This has resulted in several [far-reaching state reforms](/wiki/State_reform_in_Belgium "State reform in Belgium"), including the transition from a [unitary](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state") to [federal](/wiki/Federalism "Federalism") structure between 1970 and 1993. Tensions persist amid ongoing reforms; the country faces a strong separatist sentiment [among the Flemish](/wiki/Flemish_Movement "Flemish Movement"), controversial [language laws](/wiki/Municipalities_with_language_facilities "Municipalities with language facilities"),[[25]](#cite_note-UCL-32) and a fragmented political landscape that resulted in a record 589 days without [a government formation](/wiki/2010%E2%80%932011_Belgian_government_formation "2010–2011 Belgian government formation") following the [2010 federal election](/wiki/2010_Belgian_federal_election "2010 Belgian federal election").[[26]](#cite_note-QLUnh-33)

### Antiquity

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Map_Gallia_Tribes_Towns.png/250px-Map_Gallia_Tribes_Towns.png)](/wiki/File:Map_Gallia_Tribes_Towns.png)

*[Gallia Belgica](/wiki/Gallia_Belgica "Gallia Belgica")* at the time of [Julius Caesar](/wiki/Julius_Caesar "Julius Caesar")'s conquest of [Gaul](/wiki/Gaul "Gaul") in 54 BCE

In [his account](/wiki/Commentarii_de_Bello_Gallico "Commentarii de Bello Gallico") of the Roman invasion of [Gaul](/wiki/Gaul "Gaul"), Roman leader [Julius Caesar](/wiki/Julius_Caesar "Julius Caesar") noted that the original [Belgae](/wiki/Belgae "Belgae") inhabited the northern part of Gaul extending from the [Seine](/wiki/Seine "Seine") and [Marne](/wiki/Marne_(river) "Marne (river)") rivers to the [Rhine](/wiki/Rhine "Rhine").[[27]](#cite_note-34) They therefore inhabited most of modern Belgium, and significant parts of [France](/wiki/France "France"), the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"), and [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"). He described the Belgae as the bravest of the Gauls, and attributed this to their distance from the [Roman Province](/wiki/Roman_province "Roman province"), minimal contact with merchants importing luxury goods, and their continual warfare with the neighboring [Germanic tribes](/wiki/Germanic_peoples "Germanic peoples") across the Rhine.[[28]](#cite_note-35)[[29]](#cite_note-36) Within this broad region, Caesar referred to a specific area as "Belgium," which was politically dominant and located in what is now northern France.[[30]](#cite_note-37)

Modern Belgium, along with neighboring regions of the Netherlands and Germany, corresponds to the territories of the northernmost Belgae tribes, including the [Morini](/wiki/Morini "Morini"),[[h]](#cite_note-39) [Menapii](/wiki/Menapii "Menapii"),[[i]](#cite_note-41) [Nervii](/wiki/Nervii "Nervii"),[[j]](#cite_note-43) [Germani Cisrhenani](/wiki/Germani_cisrhenani "Germani cisrhenani"),[[k]](#cite_note-45) and [Aduatuci](/wiki/Atuatuci "Atuatuci").[[l]](#cite_note-46) Julius Caesar described these tribes as particularly warlike and economically undeveloped, noting their kinship with the Germanic tribes east of the Rhine.[[35]](#cite_note-47) Additionally, the area around [Arlon](/wiki/Arlon "Arlon") in southern Belgium was part of the territory of the powerful [Treveri](/wiki/Treveri "Treveri") tribe, whose lands extended into present-day Luxembourg and adjacent regions of France and Germany.[[36]](#cite_note-48)

Following Caesar's conquests, Gallia Belgica became the [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") name for a large Roman province encompassing most of [Northern Gaul](/wiki/Gaul "Gaul"), including the lands of the Belgae and Treveri. Subsequently, Roman Belgica was divided into two parts, and the regions closer to the lower Rhine frontier, such as the eastern part of modern Belgium, were incorporated into the frontier province of [Germania Inferior](/wiki/Germania_Inferior "Germania Inferior").[[37]](#cite_note-49)[[38]](#cite_note-50) As the central government of the [Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Western_Roman_Empire "Western Roman Empire") collapsed, the provinces of Belgica and Germania were inhabited by a mix of Romanized populations and Germanic-speaking [Franks](/wiki/Franks "Franks"), who came to dominate the military and political spheres.[[39]](#cite_note-51)

### Middle Ages

During the 5th century, the area came under the rule of the Frankish [Merovingian](/wiki/Merovingian_dynasty "Merovingian dynasty") kings, who initially established a kingdom ruling over the Romanized population in what is now northern France, and then conquered the other Frankish kingdoms. During the 8th century, the empire of the Franks came to be ruled by the [Carolingian dynasty](/wiki/Carolingian_dynasty "Carolingian dynasty"), whose centre of power included the area which is now eastern Belgium.[[40]](#cite_note-52) Over the centuries, it was divided up in many ways, but the [Treaty of Verdun](/wiki/Treaty_of_Verdun "Treaty of Verdun") in 843 divided the Carolingian Empire into three kingdoms whose borders had a lasting impact on medieval political boundaries. Most of modern Belgium was in the [Middle Kingdom](/wiki/Middle_Francia "Middle Francia"), later known as [Lotharingia](/wiki/Lotharingia "Lotharingia"), but the coastal [county of Flanders](/wiki/County_of_Flanders "County of Flanders"), west of the [Scheldt](/wiki/Scheldt "Scheldt"), became the northernmost part of [West Francia](/wiki/West_Francia "West Francia"), the predecessor of [France](/wiki/France "France"). In 870 in the [Treaty of Meerssen](/wiki/Treaty_of_Meerssen "Treaty of Meerssen"), modern Belgium lands all became part of the western kingdom for a period, but in 880 in the [Treaty of Ribemont](/wiki/Treaty_of_Ribemont "Treaty of Ribemont"), Lotharingia came under the lasting control of the eastern kingdom, which became the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire"). The lordships and bishoprics along the "March" (frontier) between the two great kingdoms maintained important connections between each other. For example, the county of Flanders expanded over the Scheldt into the empire, and during several periods was ruled by the same lords as the [county of Hainaut](/wiki/County_of_Hainaut "County of Hainaut").

In the 13th and 14th centuries, the cloth industry and commerce boomed especially in the County of Flanders and it became one of the richest areas in Europe. This prosperity played a role in conflicts between Flanders and the [king of France](/wiki/King_of_France "King of France"). Famously, Flemish militias scored a surprise victory at the [Battle of the Golden Spurs](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Golden_Spurs "Battle of the Golden Spurs") against a strong force of mounted knights in 1302, but France soon regained control of the rebellious province.

### Burgundian and Habsburg Netherlands

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Karte-Haus-Burgund_4-FR.png/250px-Karte-Haus-Burgund_4-FR.png)](/wiki/File:Karte-Haus-Burgund_4-FR.png)

The [Burgundian State](/wiki/Burgundian_Netherlands "Burgundian Netherlands") of [Charles the Bold](/wiki/Charles_the_Bold "Charles the Bold") in the 15th century

In the 15th century, the [Duke of Burgundy](/wiki/Duke_of_Burgundy "Duke of Burgundy") in France took control of Flanders, and from there [proceeded to unite](/wiki/Burgundian_inheritance_in_the_Low_Countries "Burgundian inheritance in the Low Countries") much of what is now the Benelux, the so-called [Burgundian Netherlands](/wiki/Burgundian_Netherlands "Burgundian Netherlands").[[41]](#cite_note-Wl7Nu-53) "Burgundy" and "Flanders" were the first two common names used for the Burgundian Netherlands which was the predecessor of the Austrian Netherlands, the predecessor of modern Belgium.[[42]](#cite_note-54) The union, technically stretching between two kingdoms, gave the area economic and political stability which led to an even greater prosperity and artistic creation.

Born in Belgium, the [Habsburg](/wiki/House_of_Habsburg "House of Habsburg") Emperor [Charles V](/wiki/Charles_V,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor") was heir of the Burgundians, but also of the royal families of Austria, [Castile](/wiki/Crown_of_Castile "Crown of Castile") and [Aragon](/wiki/Crown_of_Aragon "Crown of Aragon"). With the [Pragmatic Sanction of 1549](/wiki/Pragmatic_Sanction_of_1549 "Pragmatic Sanction of 1549") he gave the [Seventeen Provinces](/wiki/Seventeen_Provinces "Seventeen Provinces") more legitimacy as a stable entity, rather than just a temporary [personal union](/wiki/Personal_union "Personal union"). He also increased the influence of these Netherlands over the [Prince-Bishopric of Liège](/wiki/Prince-Bishopric_of_Li%C3%A8ge "Prince-Bishopric of Liège"), which continued to exist as a large semi-independent enclave.[[43]](#cite_note-3kmvZ-55)

### Spanish and Austrian Netherlands

The [Eighty Years' War](/wiki/Eighty_Years%27_War "Eighty Years' War") (1568–1648) was triggered by the Spanish government's policy towards [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism"), which was becoming popular in the Low Countries. The rebellious northern [United Provinces](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic") (*Belgica Foederata* in [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"), the "Federated Netherlands") eventually separated from the [Southern Netherlands](/wiki/Southern_Netherlands "Southern Netherlands") (*Belgica Regia*, the "Royal Netherlands"). The southern part continued to be ruled successively by the [Spanish](/wiki/Habsburg_Spain "Habsburg Spain") ([Spanish Netherlands](/wiki/Spanish_Netherlands "Spanish Netherlands")) and the [Austrian](/wiki/History_of_Austria "History of Austria") [House of Habsburgs](/wiki/House_of_Habsburg "House of Habsburg") ([Austrian Netherlands](/wiki/Austrian_Netherlands "Austrian Netherlands")) and comprised most of modern Belgium. This was the theatre of several more protracted conflicts during much of the 17th and 18th centuries involving France, including the [Franco-Dutch War](/wiki/Franco-Dutch_War "Franco-Dutch War") (1672–1678), the [Nine Years' War](/wiki/Nine_Years%27_War "Nine Years' War") (1688–1697), the [War of the Spanish Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Spanish_Succession "War of the Spanish Succession") (1701–1714), and part of the [War of the Austrian Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Austrian_Succession "War of the Austrian Succession") (1740–1748).

### French Revolution and United Kingdom of the Netherlands

Following the [campaigns of 1794 in the French Revolutionary Wars](/wiki/French_Revolutionary_Wars:_Campaigns_of_1794 "French Revolutionary Wars: Campaigns of 1794"), the Low Countries – including territories that were never nominally under Habsburg rule, such as the Prince-Bishopric of Liège – were annexed by the [French First Republic](/wiki/French_First_Republic "French First Republic"), ending Austrian rule in the region. After the dissolution of the [First French Empire](/wiki/First_French_Empire "First French Empire") and the abdication of [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") following his defeat on the [battlefield of Waterloo](/wiki/Battle_of_Waterloo "Battle of Waterloo"), the [Congress of Vienna](/wiki/Congress_of_Vienna "Congress of Vienna") in 1814–15 created the [United Kingdom of the Netherlands](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_the_Netherlands "United Kingdom of the Netherlands"). This [buffer state](/wiki/Buffer_state "Buffer state"), located between the major European powers, united the former territories of the Dutch Republic, the Austrian Netherlands and the Prince-Bishopric of Liège, under King [William I of Orange](/wiki/William_I_of_the_Netherlands "William I of the Netherlands").

### Independent Belgium

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Gustave_Wappers_-_Episode_of_the_September_Days_1830%2C_on_the_Grand_Place_of_Brussels_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/250px-Gustave_Wappers_-_Episode_of_the_September_Days_1830%2C_on_the_Grand_Place_of_Brussels_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gustave_Wappers_-_Episode_of_the_September_Days_1830,_on_the_Grand_Place_of_Brussels_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

*Episode of the [Belgian Revolution](/wiki/Belgian_Revolution "Belgian Revolution") of 1830*, [Gustaf Wappers](/wiki/Egide_Charles_Gustave_Wappers "Egide Charles Gustave Wappers"), 1834

In 1830, the [Belgian Revolution](/wiki/Belgian_Revolution "Belgian Revolution") led to the re-separation of the Southern Provinces from the Netherlands and to the establishment of a Catholic and bourgeois, officially French-speaking and neutral, independent Belgium under a [provisional government](/wiki/Provisional_Government_of_Belgium "Provisional Government of Belgium") and a [national congress](/wiki/National_Congress_of_Belgium "National Congress of Belgium").[[44]](#cite_note-Dobbelaere-VoyE-56)[[45]](#cite_note-Gooch-57) Since the installation of [Leopold I](/wiki/Leopold_I_of_Belgium "Leopold I of Belgium") as king on 21 July 1831, now celebrated as Belgium's [National Day](/wiki/National_Day "National Day"), Belgium has been a [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy") and [parliamentary democracy](/wiki/Parliamentary_democracy "Parliamentary democracy"), with a [*laicist*](/wiki/Secularism_in_Belgium "Secularism in Belgium") constitution based on the [Napoleonic Code](/wiki/Napoleonic_Code "Napoleonic Code").[[46]](#cite_note-UL18v-58) Although the franchise was initially restricted, [universal suffrage](/wiki/Universal_suffrage "Universal suffrage") for men was introduced after the [general strike of 1893](/wiki/Belgian_general_strike_of_1893 "Belgian general strike of 1893") (with [plural voting](/wiki/Plural_voting "Plural voting") until 1919) and for women in 1949.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/MapBelg1832-1724.jpg/250px-MapBelg1832-1724.jpg)](/wiki/File:MapBelg1832-1724.jpg)

Map of Belgium, 1832, before the final settlement of borders in 1839

The main political parties of the 19th century were the [Catholic Party](/wiki/Catholic_Party_(Belgium) "Catholic Party (Belgium)") and the [Liberal Party](/wiki/Liberal_Party_(Belgium) "Liberal Party (Belgium)"), with the [Belgian Labour Party](/wiki/Belgian_Labour_Party "Belgian Labour Party") emerging towards the end of the 19th century. French was originally the official language used by the [nobility](/wiki/Nobility "Nobility") and the [bourgeoisie](/wiki/Bourgeoisie "Bourgeoisie"), especially after the rejection of the Dutch monarchy. French progressively lost its dominance as Dutch began to recover its status. This recognition became official in 1898, and in 1967, the parliament accepted a Dutch version of the [Constitution](/wiki/Belgian_Constitution "Belgian Constitution").[[47]](#cite_note-etpIA-59)

The [Berlin Conference](/wiki/Berlin_Conference "Berlin Conference") of 1885 ceded control of the [Congo Free State](/wiki/Congo_Free_State "Congo Free State") to [King Leopold II](/wiki/Leopold_II_of_Belgium "Leopold II of Belgium") as his private possession. From around 1900 there was growing international concern for the [extreme and savage treatment of the Congolese population under Leopold II](/wiki/Atrocities_in_the_Congo_Free_State "Atrocities in the Congo Free State"), for whom the Congo was primarily a source of revenue from ivory and rubber production.[[48]](#cite_note-Forbath-60) Many Congolese were killed by Leopold's agents for failing to meet production quotas for ivory and rubber.[[49]](#cite_note-nytimes.com-61) In 1908, this outcry led the Belgian state to assume responsibility for the government of the colony, henceforth called the [Belgian Congo](/wiki/Belgian_Congo "Belgian Congo").[[50]](#cite_note-iwqD0-62) A Belgian commission in 1919 estimated that Congo's population was half what it was in 1879.[[49]](#cite_note-nytimes.com-61)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Cheering_crowds_greet_British_troops_entering_Brussels%2C_4_September_1944._BU483.jpg/250px-Cheering_crowds_greet_British_troops_entering_Brussels%2C_4_September_1944._BU483.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cheering_crowds_greet_British_troops_entering_Brussels,_4_September_1944._BU483.jpg)

Cheering crowds greet British troops entering [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"), 4 September 1944.

[Germany invaded Belgium in August 1914](/wiki/German_invasion_of_Belgium_(1914) "German invasion of Belgium (1914)") as part of the [Schlieffen Plan](/wiki/Schlieffen_Plan "Schlieffen Plan") to attack [France](/wiki/France "France"), and much of the [Western Front](/wiki/Western_Front_(World_War_I) "Western Front (World War I)") fighting of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") occurred in western parts of the country. The opening months of the war were known as the [Rape of Belgium](/wiki/Rape_of_Belgium "Rape of Belgium") due to German excesses. Belgium assumed control of the [German colonies](/wiki/German_colonies "German colonies") of [Ruanda-Urundi](/wiki/Ruanda-Urundi "Ruanda-Urundi") (modern-day [Rwanda](/wiki/Rwanda "Rwanda") and [Burundi](/wiki/Burundi "Burundi")) during the war, and in 1924 the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations") mandated them to Belgium. In the aftermath of the First World War, Belgium annexed the [Prussian districts](/wiki/Districts_of_Prussia "Districts of Prussia") of [Eupen and Malmedy](/wiki/Eupen-Malmedy "Eupen-Malmedy") in 1925, thereby causing the presence of a German-speaking minority.

[German forces again invaded the country in May 1940](/wiki/Battle_of_Belgium "Battle of Belgium"), and 40,690 Belgians, over half of them Jews, were killed during the subsequent [occupation](/wiki/German_occupation_of_Belgium_during_World_War_II "German occupation of Belgium during World War II") and [the Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust_in_Belgium "The Holocaust in Belgium"). From September 1944 to February 1945 [the Allies liberated](/wiki/Allied_advance_from_Paris_to_the_Rhine "Allied advance from Paris to the Rhine") Belgium. After [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), [a general strike](/wiki/General_strike_against_Leopold_III_of_Belgium "General strike against Leopold III of Belgium") forced King [Leopold III](/wiki/Leopold_III_of_Belgium "Leopold III of Belgium") to abdicate in 1951 in favour of his son, [Prince Baudouin](/wiki/King_Baudouin "King Baudouin"), since [many Belgians thought he had collaborated with Germany](/wiki/Royal_Question "Royal Question") during the war.[[51]](#cite_note-Arango-63) The Belgian Congo gained independence in 1960 during the [Congo Crisis](/wiki/Congo_Crisis "Congo Crisis");[[52]](#cite_note-7TmlQ-64) Ruanda-Urundi followed with its independence two years later. Belgium joined [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") as a founding member and formed the Benelux group of nations with the Netherlands and Luxembourg.

Belgium became one of the six founding members of the [European Coal and Steel Community](/wiki/European_Coal_and_Steel_Community "European Coal and Steel Community") in 1951 and of the [European Atomic Energy Community](/wiki/European_Atomic_Energy_Community "European Atomic Energy Community") and [European Economic Community](/wiki/European_Economic_Community "European Economic Community"), established in 1957. The latter has now become the European Union, for which Belgium hosts major administrations and institutions, including the [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission"), the [Council of the European Union](/wiki/Council_of_the_European_Union "Council of the European Union") and the extraordinary and committee sessions of the [European Parliament](/wiki/European_Parliament "European Parliament").

In the 1990s, Belgium saw several large scandals notably surrounding [Marc Dutroux](/wiki/Marc_Dutroux "Marc Dutroux"), [Andre Cools](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Cools "André Cools"), the [Dioxin Affair](/wiki/Dioxin_affair "Dioxin affair"), [Agusta Scandal](/wiki/Agusta_scandal "Agusta scandal") and the murder of [Karel van Noppen](/wiki/Murder_of_Karel_Van_Noppen "Murder of Karel Van Noppen").[[53]](#cite_note-65)

## Geography

Main article: [Geography of Belgium](/wiki/Geography_of_Belgium "Geography of Belgium")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Belgium_relief_location_map.jpg/500px-Belgium_relief_location_map.jpg)](/wiki/File:Belgium_relief_location_map.jpg)

Relief map of Belgium

Belgium shares borders with [France](/wiki/France "France") (620 km; 390 mi), [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") (162/167 km), [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg "Luxembourg") (148 km; 92 mi), and the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") (450 km; 280 mi). Its total surface, including water area, is 30,689 km2 (11,849 sq mi).[[5]](#cite_note-area-5) Before 2018, its total area was believed to be 30,528 km2 (11,787 sq mi). However, when the country's statistics were measured in 2018, a new calculation method was used. Unlike previous calculations, this one included the area from the coast to the low-water line, revealing the country to be 160 km2 (62 sq mi) larger in surface area than previously thought.[[54]](#cite_note-statbel-66)[[55]](#cite_note-hetlaatste-67) Its land area alone is 30,494 km2 (11,774 sq mi).[[6]](#cite_note-land-6) It lies between latitudes 49°30' and 51°30' N, and longitudes 2°33' and 6°24' E.[[56]](#cite_note-nCHdl-68)

Belgium has three main geographical regions; the coastal plain in the northwest and the central plateau both belong to the Anglo-Belgian Basin, and the [Ardennes](/wiki/Ardennes "Ardennes") uplands in the southeast to the [Hercynian orogenic belt](/wiki/Variscan_orogeny "Variscan orogeny"). The [Paris Basin](/wiki/Paris_Basin "Paris Basin") reaches a small fourth area at Belgium's southernmost tip, [Belgian Lorraine](/wiki/Belgian_Lorraine "Belgian Lorraine").[[57]](#cite_note-KKuI7-69)

The coastal plain consists mainly of sand dunes and [polders](/wiki/Polder "Polder"). Further inland lies a smooth, slowly rising landscape irrigated by numerous waterways, with fertile valleys and the northeastern sandy plain of the [Campine](/wiki/Campine "Campine") (*Kempen*). The thickly forested hills and plateaus of the Ardennes are more rugged and rocky with caves and small [gorges](/wiki/Canyon "Canyon"). Extending westward into France, this area is eastwardly connected to the [Eifel](/wiki/Eifel "Eifel") in Germany by the [High Fens](/wiki/High_Fens "High Fens") plateau, on which the [Signal de Botrange](/wiki/Signal_de_Botrange "Signal de Botrange") forms the country's highest point at 694 m (2,277 ft).[[58]](#cite_note-vomkA-70)[[59]](#cite_note-RCKrU-71)

[The climate](/wiki/Climate_of_Belgium "Climate of Belgium") is [maritime temperate](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate") with significant precipitation in all seasons ([Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): *Cfb*), like most of northwest Europe.[[60]](#cite_note-0vPZn-72) The average temperature is lowest in January at 3 °C (37.4 °F) and highest in July at 18 °C (64.4 °F). The average precipitation per month varies between 54 mm (2.1 in) for February and April, to 78 mm (3.1 in) for July.[[61]](#cite_note-7ID7K-73) Averages for the years 2000 to 2006 show daily temperature minimums of 7 °C (44.6 °F) and maximums of 14 °C (57.2 °F) and monthly rainfall of 74 mm (2.9 in); these are about 1 °C and nearly 10 millimeters above last century's normal values, respectively.[[62]](#cite_note-statbel2-74)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Show_Your_Stripes_change_in_temperature_graphic_for_All_of_Belgium_with_bars_with_labels.png/250px-Show_Your_Stripes_change_in_temperature_graphic_for_All_of_Belgium_with_bars_with_labels.png)](/wiki/File:Show_Your_Stripes_change_in_temperature_graphic_for_All_of_Belgium_with_bars_with_labels.png)

Temperature change in Belgium, each bar represents the average temperature over that year.

[Climate change in Belgium](/wiki/Climate_change_in_Belgium "Climate change in Belgium") has caused temperatures rises and more frequent and intense [heatwaves](/wiki/Heat_wave "Heat wave"), increases in winter rainfall and decreases in snowfall.[[63]](#cite_note-75) By 2100, sea levels along the Belgian coast are projected to rise by 60 to 90 cm with a maximum potential increase of up to 200 cm in the worst-case scenario.[[64]](#cite_note-:9-76) The costs of climate change are estimated to amount to €9.5 billion a year in 2050 (2% of Belgian GDP), mainly due to extreme heat, drought and flooding, while economics gains due to milder winters amount to approximately €3 billion a year (0.65% of GDP).[[64]](#cite_note-:9-76) In 2023, Belgium emitted 106.82 million tonnes of [greenhouse gases](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas "Greenhouse gas") (around 0.2% of the global total emissions), equivalent to 9.12 tonnes per person.[[65]](#cite_note-:8-77)[[66]](#cite_note-78) The country has committed to [net zero](/wiki/Net-zero_emissions "Net-zero emissions") by 2050.[[67]](#cite_note-79)

[Phytogeographically](/wiki/Phytogeography "Phytogeography"), Belgium is shared between the Atlantic European and Central European provinces of the [Circumboreal Region](/wiki/Circumboreal_Region "Circumboreal Region") within the [Boreal Kingdom](/wiki/Boreal_Kingdom "Boreal Kingdom").[[68]](#cite_note-D9WTK-80) According to the [World Wide Fund for Nature](/wiki/World_Wide_Fund_for_Nature "World Wide Fund for Nature"), the territory of Belgium belongs to the [terrestrial ecoregions](/wiki/Terrestrial_ecoregions "Terrestrial ecoregions") of [Atlantic mixed forests](/wiki/Atlantic_mixed_forests "Atlantic mixed forests") and [Western European broadleaf forests](/wiki/Western_European_broadleaf_forests "Western European broadleaf forests").[[69]](#cite_note-DinersteinOlson2017-81)[[70]](#cite_note-kjXhG-82) Belgium had a 2018 [Forest Landscape Integrity Index](/wiki/Forest_Landscape_Integrity_Index "Forest Landscape Integrity Index") mean score of 1.36/10, ranking it 163rd globally out of 172 countries.[[71]](#cite_note-FLII-Supplementary-83) In Belgium [forest cover](/wiki/Forest_cover "Forest cover") is around 23% of the total land area, equivalent to 689,300 hectares (ha) of forest in 2020, up from 677,400 hectares (ha) in 1990. In 2020, naturally regenerating forest covered 251,200 hectares (ha) and planted forest covered 438,200 hectares (ha). For the year 2015, 47% of the forest area was reported to be under [public ownership](/wiki/State_ownership "State ownership"), 53% [private ownership](/wiki/Private_property "Private property") and 0% with ownership listed as other or unknown.[[72]](#cite_note-84)[[73]](#cite_note-85)

### Provinces

## Politics and government

Main articles: [Politics of Belgium](/wiki/Politics_of_Belgium "Politics of Belgium") and [Belgian federal government](/wiki/Belgian_federal_government "Belgian federal government")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/King_Philippe_of_Belgium_%28January_2025%29.jpg/250px-King_Philippe_of_Belgium_%28January_2025%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:King_Philippe_of_Belgium_(January_2025).jpg)

[Philippe](/wiki/Philippe_of_Belgium "Philippe of Belgium")  
[King of the Belgians](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Belgium "Monarchy of Belgium")  
since 21 July 2013

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Visit_of_Bart_De_Wever%2C_Belgian_Prime_Minister%2C_to_the_European_Commission_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Visit_of_Bart_De_Wever%2C_Belgian_Prime_Minister%2C_to_the_European_Commission_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Visit_of_Bart_De_Wever,_Belgian_Prime_Minister,_to_the_European_Commission_(cropped).jpg)

[Bart De Wever](/wiki/Bart_De_Wever "Bart De Wever")  
[Prime Minister of Belgium](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Belgium "Prime Minister of Belgium")  
since 3 February 2025

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Chart_constitution_of_Belgium_EN.svg/250px-Chart_constitution_of_Belgium_EN.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Chart_constitution_of_Belgium_EN.svg)

Chart illustrating the federal government construction of Belgium

Belgium is a [constitutional](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy"), [popular monarchy](/wiki/Popular_monarchy "Popular monarchy") and a [federal](/wiki/Federalism "Federalism") [parliamentary democracy](/wiki/Parliamentary_system "Parliamentary system"). The [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameralism "Bicameralism") [federal parliament](/wiki/Belgian_Federal_Parliament "Belgian Federal Parliament") is composed of a [Senate](/wiki/Senate_(Belgium) "Senate (Belgium)") and a [Chamber of Representatives](/wiki/Chamber_of_Representatives_(Belgium) "Chamber of Representatives (Belgium)"). The former is made up of 50 senators appointed by the parliaments of the [communities and regions](/wiki/Communities,_regions_and_language_areas_of_Belgium "Communities, regions and language areas of Belgium") and 10 [co-opted senators](/wiki/Senate_(Belgium)#Co-opted_senators "Senate (Belgium)"). Prior to 2014, most of the Senate's members were directly elected. The [Chamber](/wiki/Chambers_of_parliament "Chambers of parliament")'s 150 representatives are elected under a [proportional voting](/wiki/Proportional_voting "Proportional voting") system from 11 [electoral districts](/wiki/Arrondissements_of_Belgium "Arrondissements of Belgium"). Belgium has [compulsory voting](/wiki/Compulsory_voting "Compulsory voting") and thus maintains one of the highest rates of [voter turnout](/wiki/Voter_turnout "Voter turnout") in the world.[[75]](#cite_note-Franklin-87)

The [King](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Belgium "Monarchy of Belgium") (currently [Philippe](/wiki/Philippe_of_Belgium "Philippe of Belgium")) is the [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state"), though with limited [prerogatives](/wiki/Royal_prerogative "Royal prerogative"). He appoints ministers, including a Prime Minister, that have the confidence of the [Chamber of Representatives](/wiki/Chamber_of_Representatives_of_Belgium "Chamber of Representatives of Belgium") to form the [federal government](/wiki/Federal_government "Federal government"). The Council of Ministers is composed of no more than fifteen members. With the possible exception of the Prime Minister, the Council of Ministers is composed of an equal number of Dutch-speaking members and French-speaking members.[[76]](#cite_note-constitution1-88)
The judicial system is based on [civil law](/wiki/Civil_law_(legal_system) "Civil law (legal system)") and originates from the [Napoleonic Code](/wiki/Napoleonic_Code "Napoleonic Code"). The [Court of Cassation](/wiki/Court_of_Cassation_(Belgium) "Court of Cassation (Belgium)") is the court of last resort, with the [courts of appeal](/wiki/Court_of_appeal_(Belgium) "Court of appeal (Belgium)") one level below.[[77]](#cite_note-NJcz8-89)

### Political culture

Belgium's political institutions are complex; [most political power rests on representation of the main cultural communities](/wiki/Pillarisation "Pillarisation").[[78]](#cite_note-d9qyv-90)
Since about 1970, the significant national [Belgian political parties](/wiki/Political_parties_in_Belgium "Political parties in Belgium") have split into distinct components that mainly represent the political and linguistic interests of these communities.[[79]](#cite_note-USStateDpt-91)
The major parties in each community, though close to the political center, belong to three main groups: [Christian Democrats](/wiki/Christian_democracy "Christian democracy"), [Liberals](/wiki/Liberalism_in_Belgium "Liberalism in Belgium"), and [Social Democrats](/wiki/Social_democracy "Social democracy").[[80]](#cite_note-fYr7M-92)
Further notable parties came into being well after the middle of last century, mainly to represent [linguistic](/wiki/List_of_political_parties_in_Belgium#Linguistic_parties "List of political parties in Belgium"), nationalist, or environmental interests, and recently smaller ones of some specific liberal nature.[[79]](#cite_note-USStateDpt-91)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/Palais_de_la_Nation_%28DSC01812%29.jpg/250px-Palais_de_la_Nation_%28DSC01812%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Palais_de_la_Nation_(DSC01812).jpg)

The [Belgian Federal Parliament](/wiki/Belgian_Federal_Parliament "Belgian Federal Parliament") in [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"), one of [six different governments](/wiki/List_of_governments_in_Belgium "List of governments in Belgium") of the country

A string of Christian Democrat coalition governments from 1958 [was broken in 1999](/wiki/Belgian_federal_election,_1999 "Belgian federal election, 1999") after the first [dioxin crisis](/wiki/Dioxine_affair "Dioxine affair"), a major [food contamination](/wiki/Food_contaminant "Food contaminant") scandal.[[81]](#cite_note-bKdfB-93)[[82]](#cite_note-hjCXN-94)[[83]](#cite_note-uoINh-95) A "rainbow coalition" emerged from six parties: the Flemish and the French-speaking Liberals, Social Democrats and Greens.[[84]](#cite_note-Mbcyv-96) Later, a "[purple coalition](/wiki/Purple_(government) "Purple (government)")" of Liberals and Social Democrats formed after the Greens lost most of their seats in the [2003 election](/wiki/Belgian_federal_election,_2003 "Belgian federal election, 2003").[[85]](#cite_note-9lpYO-97)

The government led by Prime Minister [Guy Verhofstadt](/wiki/Guy_Verhofstadt "Guy Verhofstadt") from 1999 to 2007 achieved a balanced budget, some [tax reforms](/wiki/Tax_reform "Tax reform"), a labor-market reform, scheduled [nuclear phase-out](/wiki/Nuclear_energy_policy#Europe "Nuclear energy policy") and instigated legislation allowing more stringent [war crime](/wiki/War_Crimes_Law_(Belgium) "War Crimes Law (Belgium)") and more lenient [soft drug usage](/wiki/Drug_harmfulness "Drug harmfulness") prosecution. Restrictions on [euthanasia](/wiki/Euthanasia "Euthanasia") were reduced. In 2003, Belgium became one of the first countries in the world to legalise [same-sex marriage](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage_in_Belgium "Same-sex marriage in Belgium").[[86]](#cite_note-brusselstimes-98) The government promoted active [diplomacy](/wiki/Diplomacy "Diplomacy") in Africa[[87]](#cite_note-WEf4J-99) and [opposed the invasion of Iraq](/wiki/Governmental_positions_on_the_Iraq_War_prior_to_the_2003_invasion_of_Iraq "Governmental positions on the Iraq War prior to the 2003 invasion of Iraq").[[88]](#cite_note-LZ5BE-100) It is the only country that does not have age restrictions on euthanasia.[[89]](#cite_note-bbc-101)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Senate_of_Belgium_hemicycle.jpg/250px-Senate_of_Belgium_hemicycle.jpg)](/wiki/File:Senate_of_Belgium_hemicycle.jpg)

The hemicycle of the [Belgian Senate](/wiki/Senate_(Belgium) "Senate (Belgium)")

Verhofstadt's coalition fared badly in the [June 2007 elections](/wiki/Belgian_federal_election,_2007 "Belgian federal election, 2007"). For more than a year, the country experienced a [political crisis](/wiki/2007_Belgian_government_formation "2007 Belgian government formation").[[90]](#cite_note-N2qAB-102) This crisis was such that many observers speculated on a possible [partition of Belgium](/wiki/Partition_of_Belgium "Partition of Belgium").[[91]](#cite_note-SanFranChro-103)[[92]](#cite_note-BBCJuly2008-104)[[93]](#cite_note-Telegraph2010-105) From 21 December 2007 until 20 March 2008 the temporary [Verhofstadt III Government](/wiki/Verhofstadt_III_Government "Verhofstadt III Government") was in office. This was a coalition of the [Flemish](/wiki/Christen-Democratisch_en_Vlaams "Christen-Democratisch en Vlaams") and [Francophone Christian Democrats](/wiki/Humanist_Democratic_Centre "Humanist Democratic Centre"), the [Flemish](/wiki/Open_Vlaamse_Liberalen_en_Democraten "Open Vlaamse Liberalen en Democraten") and [Francophone Liberals](/wiki/Mouvement_R%C3%A9formateur "Mouvement Réformateur") together with the [Francophone Social Democrats](/wiki/Socialist_Party_(francophone_Belgium) "Socialist Party (francophone Belgium)").[[94]](#cite_note-news-106)

On that day, a [new government](/wiki/Leterme_I_Government "Leterme I Government"), led by Flemish Christian Democrat [Yves Leterme](/wiki/Yves_Leterme "Yves Leterme"), the actual winner of the federal elections of June 2007, was sworn in by the King. On 15 July 2008 Leterme offered the resignation of the cabinet to the King, as no progress in [constitutional reforms](/wiki/State_reform_in_Belgium "State reform in Belgium") had been made.[[94]](#cite_note-news-106) In December 2008, Leterme once more offered his resignation after a [crisis](/wiki/2008%E2%80%932009_Belgian_financial_crisis "2008–2009 Belgian financial crisis") surrounding the sale of [Fortis](/wiki/Fortis_(finance) "Fortis (finance)") to [BNP Paribas](/wiki/BNP_Paribas "BNP Paribas").[[95]](#cite_note-r7UJ3-107) At this juncture, his resignation was accepted and [Christian Democratic and Flemish](/wiki/Christen-Democratisch_en_Vlaams "Christen-Democratisch en Vlaams") [Herman Van Rompuy](/wiki/Herman_Van_Rompuy "Herman Van Rompuy") was sworn in as Prime Minister on 30 December 2008.[[96]](#cite_note-4GrHs-108)

After Herman Van Rompuy was designated the first permanent [President of the European Council](/wiki/President_of_the_European_Council "President of the European Council") on 19 November 2009, he offered the resignation of his government to King Albert II on 25 November 2009. A few hours later, the [new government](/wiki/Leterme_II_Government "Leterme II Government") under Prime Minister [Yves Leterme](/wiki/Yves_Leterme "Yves Leterme") was sworn in. On 22 April 2010, Leterme again offered the resignation of his cabinet to the King[[97]](#cite_note-a18xf-109) after one of the coalition partners, the [OpenVLD](/wiki/OpenVLD "OpenVLD"), withdrew from the government, and on 26 April 2010 King Albert officially accepted the resignation.[[98]](#cite_note-pFUq8-110)

The Parliamentary elections in Belgium on 13 June 2010 saw the Flemish nationalist [N-VA](/wiki/New_Flemish_Alliance "New Flemish Alliance") become the largest party in Flanders, and the Socialist Party PS the largest party in Wallonia.[[99]](#cite_note-XVrGP-111) Until December 2011, Belgium was governed by Leterme's [caretaker government](/wiki/Caretaker_government "Caretaker government") awaiting the end of the deadlocked negotiations for [formation of a new government](/wiki/2010%E2%80%932011_Belgian_government_formation "2010–2011 Belgian government formation"). By 30 March 2011, this set a new world record for the elapsed time without an official government, previously held by war-torn [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq").[[100]](#cite_note-BBC_News_6_May_2015-112) Finally, in December 2011 the [Di Rupo Government](/wiki/Di_Rupo_Government "Di Rupo Government") led by Walloon socialist Prime Minister [Elio Di Rupo](/wiki/Elio_Di_Rupo "Elio Di Rupo") was sworn in.[[101]](#cite_note-PXMRM-113)

The [2014 federal election](/wiki/Belgian_federal_election,_2014 "Belgian federal election, 2014") (coinciding with the [regional elections](/wiki/Belgian_regional_elections,_2014 "Belgian regional elections, 2014")) resulted in a further electoral gain for the Flemish nationalist N-VA. However, the incumbent coalition (composed of Flemish and French-speaking Social Democrats, Liberals, and Christian Democrats) maintained a solid majority in Parliament and all electoral constituencies. On 22 July 2014, King Philippe nominated [Charles Michel](/wiki/Charles_Michel "Charles Michel") (MR) and [Kris Peeters](/wiki/Kris_Peeters "Kris Peeters") (CD&V) to [lead the formation of a new federal cabinet](/wiki/2014_Belgian_government_formation "2014 Belgian government formation") composed of the Flemish parties N-VA, CD&V, Open Vld and the French-speaking MR, which resulted in the [Michel Government](/wiki/Michel_I_Government "Michel I Government"). It was the first time N-VA was part of the federal cabinet, while the French-speaking side was represented only by the MR, which achieved a minority of the public votes in Wallonia.[[102]](#cite_note-wMR0S-114)

In May [2019 federal elections](/wiki/2019_Belgian_federal_election "2019 Belgian federal election") in the Flemish-speaking northern region of Flanders, the far-right [Vlaams Belang](/wiki/Vlaams_Belang "Vlaams Belang") party made major gains. In the French-speaking southern area of Wallonia, the Socialists were strong. The moderate Flemish nationalist party, the N-VA, remained the largest party in parliament.[[103]](#cite_note-ftZtr-115) In July 2019, Prime Minister [Charles Michel](/wiki/Charles_Michel "Charles Michel") was selected to hold the post of President of the [European Council](/wiki/European_Council "European Council").[[104]](#cite_note-j3g6O-116) His successor [Sophie Wilmès](/wiki/Sophie_Wilm%C3%A8s "Sophie Wilmès") was Belgium's first female prime minister. She led the caretaker government since October 2019.[[105]](#cite_note-2hC0Z-117) The Flemish Liberal party politician [Alexander De Croo](/wiki/Alexander_De_Croo "Alexander De Croo") became the new prime minister in October 2020. The parties had agreed on the federal government 16 months after the elections.[[106]](#cite_note-yD8q7-118)

On 3 February 2025, [Bart De Wever](/wiki/Bart_De_Wever "Bart De Wever"), leader of the [New Flemish Alliance](/wiki/New_Flemish_Alliance "New Flemish Alliance"), became the new prime minister. He is the first [Flemish nationalist](/wiki/Flemish_nationalist "Flemish nationalist") to be named Belgian premier.[[107]](#cite_note-119)

### Communities and regions

Main article: [Communities, regions and language areas of Belgium](/wiki/Communities,_regions_and_language_areas_of_Belgium "Communities, regions and language areas of Belgium")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Communities_of_Belgium.svg/250px-Communities_of_Belgium.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Communities_of_Belgium.svg)

Communities:  

[Flemish Community](/wiki/Flemish_Community "Flemish Community") / Dutch language area

Flemish & French Community / bilingual language area

[French Community](/wiki/French_Community_of_Belgium "French Community of Belgium") / French language area

[German-speaking Community](/wiki/German-speaking_Community_of_Belgium "German-speaking Community of Belgium") / German language area

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Regions_of_Belgium.svg/250px-Regions_of_Belgium.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Regions_of_Belgium.svg)

Regions:  

[Flemish Region](/wiki/Flemish_Region "Flemish Region") / Dutch language area

[Brussels-Capital Region](/wiki/Brussels-Capital_Region "Brussels-Capital Region") / bilingual area

[Walloon Region](/wiki/Wallonia "Wallonia") / French and German language areas

Following a usage which can be traced back to the Burgundian and Habsburg courts,[[108]](#cite_note-zZuRK-120) in the 19th century it was necessary to speak French to belong to the governing upper class, and those who could only speak Dutch were effectively second-class citizens.[[109]](#cite_note-ii8cr-121) Late that century, and continuing into the 20th century, [Flemish movements](/wiki/Flemish_movement "Flemish movement") evolved to counter this situation.[[110]](#cite_note-Fitzmaurice1996-p31-122)

While the people in Southern Belgium spoke French or dialects of French, and [most Brusselers adopted French](/wiki/Frenchification_of_Brussels "Frenchification of Brussels") as their first language, the Flemings refused to do so and succeeded progressively in making Dutch an equal language in the education system.[[110]](#cite_note-Fitzmaurice1996-p31-122) Following World War II, Belgian politics became increasingly dominated by the autonomy of its two main linguistic communities.[[111]](#cite_note-EED-123) Intercommunal tensions rose and the constitution was amended to minimize the potential for conflict.[[111]](#cite_note-EED-123)

Based on the four language areas defined in 1962–63 (the Dutch, bilingual, French and German language areas), consecutive [revisions](/wiki/State_reform_in_Belgium "State reform in Belgium") of [the country's constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Belgium "Constitution of Belgium") in 1970, 1980, 1988 and 1993 established a unique form of a federal state with segregated political power into three levels:[[112]](#cite_note-rolandwillemyns-124)[[113]](#cite_note-constitution2-125)

1. The [federal government](/wiki/Belgian_federal_government "Belgian federal government"), based in Brussels.
2. The three language communities:
   * the [Flemish Community](/wiki/Flemish_Community "Flemish Community") (Dutch-speaking);
   * the [French Community](/wiki/French_Community_of_Belgium "French Community of Belgium") (French-speaking);[[m]](#cite_note-128)
   * the [German-speaking Community](/wiki/German-speaking_Community_of_Belgium "German-speaking Community of Belgium").
3. The three regions:
   * the [Flemish Region](/wiki/Flemish_Region "Flemish Region"), subdivided into five [provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_Belgium "Provinces of Belgium");
   * the [Walloon Region](/wiki/Wallonia "Wallonia"), subdivided into five provinces;
   * the [Brussels-Capital Region](/wiki/Brussels-Capital_Region "Brussels-Capital Region").

The [constitutional language areas](/wiki/Language_legislation_in_Belgium "Language legislation in Belgium") determine the official languages in their municipalities, as well as the geographical limits of the empowered institutions for specific matters.[[116]](#cite_note-Fitzmaurice1996-p121-129) Although this would allow for seven parliaments and governments when the Communities and Regions were created in 1980, Flemish politicians decided to merge both.[[117]](#cite_note-Fitzmaurice1996-p122-130) Thus the Flemings just have one single institutional body of parliament and government is empowered for all except federal and specific municipal matters.[[n]](#cite_note-131)

The overlapping boundaries of the Regions and Communities have created two notable peculiarities: the territory of the Brussels-Capital Region (which came into existence nearly a decade after the other regions) is included in both the Flemish and French Communities, and the territory of the German-speaking Community lies wholly within the Walloon Region. Conflicts about jurisdiction between the bodies are resolved by the [Constitutional Court of Belgium](/wiki/Constitutional_Court_of_Belgium "Constitutional Court of Belgium"). The structure is intended as a compromise to allow different cultures to live together peacefully.[[19]](#cite_note-Fitzmaurice-25)

### Locus of policy jurisdiction

The Federal State's authority includes justice, defense, federal police, social security, nuclear energy, monetary policy and public debt, and other aspects of public finances. State-owned companies include the [Belgian Post Group](/wiki/Belgian_Post_Group "Belgian Post Group") and [Belgian Railways](/wiki/National_Railway_Company_of_Belgium "National Railway Company of Belgium"). The Federal Government is responsible for the obligations of Belgium and its federalized institutions towards the European Union and NATO. It controls substantial parts of public health, home affairs and foreign affairs.[[118]](#cite_note-fedgov1-132) The budget—without the debt—controlled by the federal government amounts to about 50% of the national fiscal income. The federal government employs around 12% of the civil servants.[[119]](#cite_note-3JE9u-133)

Communities exercise their authority only within linguistically determined geographical boundaries, originally oriented towards the individuals of a Community's language: culture (including audiovisual media), education and the use of the relevant language. Extensions to personal matters less directly connected with language comprise health policy (curative and preventive medicine) and assistance to individuals (protection of youth, social welfare, aid to families, [immigrant](/wiki/Immigrant "Immigrant") assistance services, and so on.).[[120]](#cite_note-fedgov2-134)

Regions have authority in fields that can be broadly associated with their territory. These include economy, employment, agriculture, water policy, housing, public works, energy, transport, the environment, town and country planning, nature conservation, credit and foreign trade. They supervise the provinces, municipalities and intercommunal utility companies.[[121]](#cite_note-fedgov3-135)

In several fields, the different levels each have their own say on specifics. With education, for instance, the autonomy of the Communities neither includes decisions about the compulsory aspect nor allows for setting minimum requirements for awarding qualifications, which remain federal matters.[[118]](#cite_note-fedgov1-132) Each level of government can be involved in scientific research and international relations associated with its powers. The treaty-making power of the Regions' and Communities' Governments is the broadest of all the Federating units of all the Federations all over the world.[[122]](#cite_note-Lagasse1-136)[[123]](#cite_note-Lagasse2-137)[[124]](#cite_note-Suinen-138)

### Foreign relations

Main article: [Foreign relations of Belgium](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Belgium "Foreign relations of Belgium")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Belgique_-_Bruxelles_-_Schuman_-_Berlaymont_-_01.jpg/250px-Belgique_-_Bruxelles_-_Schuman_-_Berlaymont_-_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Belgique_-_Bruxelles_-_Schuman_-_Berlaymont_-_01.jpg)

The [Berlaymont building](/wiki/Berlaymont_building "Berlaymont building") in Brussels, seat of the [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission")

Because of its location at the crossroads of [Western Europe](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe"), Belgium has historically been the route of invading armies from its larger neighbors. With virtually defenseless borders, Belgium has traditionally sought to avoid domination by the more powerful nations which surround it through a policy of mediation. The Belgians have been strong advocates of [European integration](/wiki/European_integration "European integration"). The headquarters of [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") and of several of the institutions of the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") are located in Belgium.

### Armed forces

Main article: [Belgian Armed Forces](/wiki/Belgian_Armed_Forces "Belgian Armed Forces")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/FA-109_%2850064453591%29.jpg/250px-FA-109_%2850064453591%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:FA-109_(50064453591).jpg)

[F-16 Fighting Falcon](/wiki/General_Dynamics_F-16_Fighting_Falcon "General Dynamics F-16 Fighting Falcon") of the Belgian Air Component

The Belgian Armed Forces had 23,200 active personnel in 2023, including 8,500 in the [Land Component](/wiki/Belgian_Land_Component "Belgian Land Component"), 1,400 in the [Naval Component](/wiki/Belgian_Naval_Component "Belgian Naval Component"), 4,900 in the [Air Component](/wiki/Belgian_Air_Component "Belgian Air Component"), 1,450 in the [Medical Component](/wiki/Belgian_Medical_Component "Belgian Medical Component"), and 6,950 in joint service, in addition to 5,900 reserve personnel.[[125]](#cite_note-iiss2023-139) In 2019, Belgium's defense budget totaled €4.303 billion ($4.921 billion) representing .93% of its GDP.[[126]](#cite_note-R8Zsp-140) The operational commands of the four components are subordinate to the Staff Department for Operations and Training of the [Ministry of Defense](/wiki/Ministry_of_Defence_(Belgium) "Ministry of Defence (Belgium)"), which is headed by the Assistant Chief of Staff Operations and Training, and to the [Chief of Defense](/wiki/Chief_of_Defence_(Belgium) "Chief of Defence (Belgium)").[[127]](#cite_note-LAP2a-141) The Belgian military consists of volunteers (conscription was abolished in 1995), and citizens of other EU states, Iceland, Norway, Switzerland, or Lichtenstein are also able to join. Belgium has troops deployed in several African countries as part of UN or EU missions, in Iraq for the [war against the Islamic State](/wiki/War_against_the_Islamic_State "War against the Islamic State"), and in eastern Europe for the NATO presence there.[[125]](#cite_note-iiss2023-139)[[128]](#cite_note-142)

The effects of the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War") made [collective security](/wiki/Collective_security "Collective security") a priority for [Belgian foreign policy](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Belgium "Foreign relations of Belgium"). In March 1948 Belgium signed the [Treaty of Brussels](/wiki/Treaty_of_Brussels "Treaty of Brussels") and then joined [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") in 1948. However, the integration of the armed forces into NATO did not begin until after the [Korean War](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War").[[129]](#cite_note-HBglz-143) The Belgians, along with the Luxembourg government, sent a detachment of battalion strength to fight in Korea known as the [Belgian United Nations Command](/wiki/Belgian_United_Nations_Command "Belgian United Nations Command"). This mission was the first in a long line of UN missions which the Belgians supported. Currently, the Belgian Marine Component is working closely together with the [Dutch Navy](/wiki/Dutch_Navy "Dutch Navy") under the command of the [Admiral Benelux](/wiki/Admiral_Benelux "Admiral Benelux").

According to the 2024 [Global Peace Index](/wiki/Global_Peace_Index "Global Peace Index"), Belgium is the 16th most peaceful country in the world.[[130]](#cite_note-144)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Belgium](/wiki/Economy_of_Belgium "Economy of Belgium")

Belgium's strongly globalized economy[[131]](#cite_note-rlY6Y-145) and its [transport infrastructure](/wiki/Transport_in_Belgium "Transport in Belgium") are integrated with the rest of Europe. Its location at the heart of a highly industrialized region helped make it the world's 15th largest trading nation in 2007.[[132]](#cite_note-29KnH-146)[[133]](#cite_note-6qStY-147) The economy is characterized by a highly productive work force, high [GNP](/wiki/Gross_national_product "Gross national product") and high exports per capita.[[134]](#cite_note-diplomatbe1-148) Belgium's main imports are raw materials, machinery and equipment, chemicals, raw diamonds, pharmaceuticals, foodstuffs, transportation equipment, and oil products. Its main exports are machinery and equipment, chemicals, finished diamonds, metals and metal products, and foodstuffs.[[135]](#cite_note-CIA-149)

The Belgian economy is heavily service-oriented and shows a dual nature: a dynamic Flemish economy and a Walloon economy that lags behind.[[19]](#cite_note-Fitzmaurice-25)[[136]](#cite_note-SvlJ5-150)[[o]](#cite_note-151) One of the founding members of the European Union, Belgium strongly supports an [open economy](/wiki/Open_economy "Open economy") and the extension of the powers of EU institutions to integrate member economies. Since 1922, through the [Belgium-Luxembourg Economic Union](/wiki/Belgium-Luxembourg_Economic_Union "Belgium-Luxembourg Economic Union"), Belgium and Luxembourg have been a single trade market with customs and currency union.[[137]](#cite_note-PnEgr-152)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Ougr%C3%A9e_Cokerie.jpg/250px-Ougr%C3%A9e_Cokerie.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ougr%C3%A9e_Cokerie.jpg)

Steelmaking along the [Meuse](/wiki/Meuse "Meuse") at [Ougrée](/wiki/Ougr%C3%A9e "Ougrée"), near [Liège](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge "Liège")

Belgium was the first continental European country to undergo the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"), in the early 19th century.[[138]](#cite_note-erih-153) Areas in [Liège Province](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge_Province "Liège Province") and around [Charleroi](/wiki/Charleroi "Charleroi") rapidly developed mining and steelmaking, which flourished until the mid-20th century in the [Sambre and Meuse valley](/wiki/Sillon_industriel "Sillon industriel") and made Belgium one of the three most industrialized nations in the world from 1830 to 1910.[[139]](#cite_note-jwR7A-154)[[140]](#cite_note-Q0dyY-155) However, by the 1840s the textile industry of Flanders was in severe crisis, and the region experienced [famine](/wiki/European_Potato_Failure "European Potato Failure") from 1846 to 1850.[[141]](#cite_note-WYx8x-156)[[142]](#cite_note-Gynyt-157)

After World War II, [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent") and [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp") experienced a rapid expansion of the chemical and petroleum industries. The 1973 and [1979 oil crises](/wiki/1979_oil_crisis "1979 oil crisis") sent the economy into a recession; it was particularly prolonged in Wallonia, where the steel industry had become less competitive and experienced a serious decline.[[79]](#cite_note-USStateDpt-91) In the 1980s and 1990s, the economic center of the country continued to shift northwards and is now concentrated in the populous [Flemish Diamond](/wiki/Flemish_Diamond "Flemish Diamond") area.[[143]](#cite_note-THRZa-158)

By the end of the 1980s, Belgian [macroeconomic policies](/wiki/Macroeconomic_policies "Macroeconomic policies") had resulted in a cumulative government debt of about 120% of GDP. As of 2006[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Belgium&action=edit), the budget was balanced and public debt was equal to 90.30% of GDP.[[144]](#cite_note-UwQoK-159) In 2005 and 2006, real GDP growth rates of 1.5% and 3.0%, respectively, were slightly above the average for the Euro area. Unemployment rates of 8.4% in 2005 and 8.2% in 2006 were close to the area average. By October 2010, this had grown to 8.5% compared to an average rate of 9.6% for the European Union as a whole (EU 27).[[145]](#cite_note-nbb-160)[[146]](#cite_note-jWtuA-161) From 1832 until 2002, Belgium's currency was the [Belgian franc](/wiki/Belgian_franc "Belgian franc"). Belgium switched to the euro in 2002, with the first sets of euro coins being minted in 1999. The standard [Belgian euro coins](/wiki/Belgian_euro_coins "Belgian euro coins") designated for circulation show the portrait of the monarch (first King Albert II, since 2013 King Philippe).

Despite an 18% decrease observed from 1970 to 1999, Belgium still had in 1999 the highest [rail network](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Belgium "Rail transport in Belgium") density within the European Union with 113.8 km/1 000 km2. On the other hand, the same period, 1970–1999, has seen a huge growth (+56%) of the [motorway network](/wiki/List_of_motorways_in_Belgium "List of motorways in Belgium"). In 1999, the density of km motorways per 1000 km2 and 1000 inhabitants amounted to 55.1 and 16.5 respectively and were significantly superior to the EU's means of 13.7 and 15.9.[[147]](#cite_note-panorama-162)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Port_cranes_at_Zeebr%C3%BCgge%2C_Belgium_2.JPG/250px-Port_cranes_at_Zeebr%C3%BCgge%2C_Belgium_2.JPG)](/wiki/File:Port_cranes_at_Zeebr%C3%BCgge,_Belgium_2.JPG)

[Port of Zeebrugge](/wiki/Port_of_Zeebrugge "Port of Zeebrugge")

From a biological resource perspective, Belgium has a low endowment: Belgium's [biocapacity](/wiki/Biocapacity "Biocapacity") adds up to only 0.8 global hectares in 2016,[[148]](#cite_note-footprintdata-163) just about half of the 1.6 global hectares of biocapacity available per person worldwide.[[149]](#cite_note-TcuGH-164) In contrast, in 2016, Belgians used on average 6.3 global hectares of biocapacity - their [ecological footprint](/wiki/Ecological_footprint "Ecological footprint") of consumption. This means they required about eight times as much biocapacity as Belgium contains. As a result, Belgium was running a biocapacity deficit of 5.5 global hectares per person in 2016.[[148]](#cite_note-footprintdata-163)

Belgium experiences some of the most congested traffic in Europe. In 2010, commuters to the cities of Brussels and Antwerp spent respectively 65 and 64 hours a year in traffic jams.[[150]](#cite_note-wxOHJ-165) Like in most small European countries, more than 80% of the airways traffic is handled by a single airport, the [Brussels Airport](/wiki/Brussels_Airport "Brussels Airport"). The [ports of Antwerp](/wiki/Port_of_Antwerp "Port of Antwerp") and [Zeebrugge (Bruges)](/wiki/Port_of_Zeebrugge "Port of Zeebrugge") share more than 80% of Belgian maritime traffic, Antwerp being the second European harbor with a gross weight of goods handled of 115 988 000 t in 2000 after a growth of 10.9% over the preceding five years.[[147]](#cite_note-panorama-162)[[151]](#cite_note-xrHdp-166) In 2016, the port of Antwerp handled 214 million tons after a year-on-year growth of 2.7%.[[152]](#cite_note-IokZW-167)

There is a large economic gap between [Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders") and [Wallonia](/wiki/Wallonia "Wallonia"). Wallonia was historically wealthy compared to Flanders, mostly due to its [heavy industries](/wiki/Heavy_industry "Heavy industry"), but the decline of the steel industry post-World War II led to the region's rapid decline, whereas Flanders rose swiftly. Since then, Flanders has been prosperous, among the wealthiest regions in Europe, whereas Wallonia has been languishing. As of 2007, the unemployment rate of Wallonia is over double that of Flanders. The divide has played a key part in the tensions between the Flemish and Walloons in addition to the already-existing language divide. [Pro-independence movements](/wiki/Flemish_Movement "Flemish Movement") have gained high popularity in Flanders as a consequence. The separatist [New Flemish Alliance](/wiki/New_Flemish_Alliance "New Flemish Alliance") (N-VA) party, for instance, is the largest party in Belgium.[[153]](#cite_note-Crisis-168)[[154]](#cite_note-Divided-169)[[155]](#cite_note-google-170)

### Science and technology

Further information: [Science and technology in Brussels](/wiki/Science_and_technology_in_Brussels "Science and technology in Brussels"), [Science and technology in Flanders](/wiki/Science_and_technology_in_Flanders "Science and technology in Flanders"), and [Science and technology in Wallonia](/wiki/Science_and_technology_in_Wallonia "Science and technology in Wallonia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Mercator.jpg/250px-Mercator.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mercator.jpg)

[Gerardus Mercator](/wiki/Gerardus_Mercator "Gerardus Mercator")

Contributions to the development of science and technology have appeared throughout the country's history. The 16th century [Early Modern](/wiki/Early_Modern "Early Modern") flourishing of Western Europe included [cartographer](/wiki/Cartography "Cartography") [Gerardus Mercator](/wiki/Gerardus_Mercator "Gerardus Mercator"), [anatomist](/wiki/Anatomy "Anatomy") [Andreas Vesalius](/wiki/Andreas_Vesalius "Andreas Vesalius"), [herbalist](/wiki/Herbalist "Herbalist") [Rembert Dodoens](/wiki/Rembert_Dodoens "Rembert Dodoens")[[156]](#cite_note-dQGxf-171)[[157]](#cite_note-kXPyM-172)[[158]](#cite_note-hV1er-173)[[159]](#cite_note-vjZwZ-174) and [mathematician](/wiki/Mathematician "Mathematician") [Simon Stevin](/wiki/Simon_Stevin "Simon Stevin") among the most influential scientists.[[160]](#cite_note-jnhYL-175)

Chemist [Ernest Solvay](/wiki/Ernest_Solvay "Ernest Solvay")[[161]](#cite_note-zHHZT-176) and engineer [Zenobe Gramme](/wiki/Zenobe_Gramme "Zenobe Gramme") (École industrielle de Liège)[[162]](#cite_note-ec0kr-177) gave their names to the [Solvay process](/wiki/Solvay_process "Solvay process") and the [Gramme dynamo](/wiki/Gramme_dynamo "Gramme dynamo"), respectively, in the 1860s. [Bakelite](/wiki/Bakelite "Bakelite") was developed in 1907–1909 by [Leo Baekeland](/wiki/Leo_Baekeland "Leo Baekeland"). Ernest Solvay also acted as a major philanthropist and gave his name to the [Solvay Institute of Sociology](/wiki/Solvay_Institute_of_Sociology "Solvay Institute of Sociology"), the [Solvay Brussels School of Economics and Management](/wiki/Solvay_Brussels_School_of_Economics_and_Management "Solvay Brussels School of Economics and Management") and the International Solvay Institutes for Physics and Chemistry which are now part of the [Université libre de Bruxelles](/wiki/Universit%C3%A9_libre_de_Bruxelles "Université libre de Bruxelles"). In 1911, he started a series of conferences, the [Solvay Conferences](/wiki/Solvay_Conference "Solvay Conference") on Physics and Chemistry, which have had a deep impact on the evolution of quantum physics and chemistry.[[163]](#cite_note-VJ9RO-178) A major contribution to fundamental science was also due to a Belgian, [Monsignor](/wiki/Monsignor "Monsignor") [Georges Lemaître](/wiki/Georges_Lema%C3%AEtre "Georges Lemaître") ([Catholic University of Louvain](/wiki/Catholic_University_of_Leuven_(1834%E2%80%931968) "Catholic University of Leuven (1834–1968)")), who is credited with proposing the [Big Bang](/wiki/Big_Bang "Big Bang") theory of the origin of the universe in 1927.[[164]](#cite_note-Eq42c-179)

Three [Nobel Prizes in Physiology or Medicine](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physiology_or_Medicine "Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine") were awarded to Belgians: [Jules Bordet](/wiki/Jules_Bordet "Jules Bordet") (Université libre de Bruxelles) in 1919, [Corneille Heymans](/wiki/Corneille_Heymans "Corneille Heymans") ([University of Ghent](/wiki/University_of_Ghent "University of Ghent")) in 1938 and [Albert Claude](/wiki/Albert_Claude "Albert Claude") (Université libre de Bruxelles) together with [Christian de Duve](/wiki/Christian_de_Duve "Christian de Duve") ([Université catholique de Louvain](/wiki/Universit%C3%A9_catholique_de_Louvain "Université catholique de Louvain")) in 1974. [François Englert](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Englert "François Englert") (Université libre de Bruxelles) was awarded the [Nobel Prize in Physics](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physics "Nobel Prize in Physics") in 2013. [Ilya Prigogine](/wiki/Ilya_Prigogine "Ilya Prigogine") (Université libre de Bruxelles) was awarded the [Nobel Prize in Chemistry](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Chemistry "Nobel Prize in Chemistry") in 1977.[[165]](#cite_note-dXPik-180) Two Belgian mathematicians have been awarded the [Fields Medal](/wiki/Fields_Medal "Fields Medal"): [Pierre Deligne](/wiki/Pierre_Deligne "Pierre Deligne") in 1978 and [Jean Bourgain](/wiki/Jean_Bourgain "Jean Bourgain") in 1994.[[166]](#cite_note-AdIxU-181)[[167]](#cite_note-zjTQ6-182) Belgium was ranked 24th in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index") in 2024,[[168]](#cite_note-183) and its ranking advanced to 21st in the index of 2025[[169]](#cite_note-184)[[170]](#cite_note-185)

## Demographics

Main articles: [Demographics of Belgium](/wiki/Demographics_of_Belgium "Demographics of Belgium") and [Belgians](/wiki/Belgians "Belgians")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Population_density_in_Belgium.png/500px-Population_density_in_Belgium.png)](/wiki/File:Population_density_in_Belgium.png)

Population density in Belgium by [arrondissement](/wiki/Arrondissements_of_Belgium "Arrondissements of Belgium")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/00_Bruxelles_-_Mont_des_Arts.jpg/250px-00_Bruxelles_-_Mont_des_Arts.jpg)](/wiki/File:00_Bruxelles_-_Mont_des_Arts.jpg)

Brussels, the capital city and largest metropolitan area of Belgium

As of 1 January 2024, the total population of Belgium according to its population register was 11,763,650.[[8]](#cite_note-population-8) The population density of Belgium is 383/km2 (990/sq mi) as of January 2024, making it the [22nd most densely populated](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_population_density "List of countries and dependencies by population density") country in the world, and the [6th most densely populated](/wiki/Area_and_population_of_European_countries "Area and population of European countries") country in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"). The most densely populated province is [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp_province "Antwerp province"), the least densely populated province is [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg_(Belgium) "Luxembourg (Belgium)"). As of January 2024, the [Flemish Region](/wiki/Flemish_Region "Flemish Region") (Flanders) had a population of 6,821,770 (58.0% of Belgium), its most populous cities being [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp") (545,000), [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent") (270,000), and [Bruges](/wiki/Bruges "Bruges") (120,000). The [Walloon Region](/wiki/Walloon_Region "Walloon Region") (Wallonia) had a population of 3,692,283 (31.4% of Belgium), its most populous cities being [Charleroi](/wiki/Charleroi "Charleroi") (204,000), [Liège](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge "Liège") (196,000), and [Namur](/wiki/Namur "Namur") (114,000). The [Brussels-Capital Region](/wiki/Brussels-Capital_Region "Brussels-Capital Region") (Brussels) had a population of 1,249,597 (10.6% of Belgium), consisting of [19 municipalities](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_of_the_Brussels-Capital_Region "List of municipalities of the Brussels-Capital Region"), its most populous cities being the [City of Brussels](/wiki/City_of_Brussels "City of Brussels") (197,000), [Schaerbeek](/wiki/Schaerbeek "Schaerbeek") (130,000), and [Anderlecht](/wiki/Anderlecht "Anderlecht") (127,000).[[8]](#cite_note-population-8)

In 2023 the average [total fertility rate](/wiki/Total_fertility_rate "Total fertility rate") (TFR) across Belgium was 1.38 children per woman, below the replacement rate of 2.1; it remains considerably below the high of 4.87 children born per woman in 1873.[[171]](#cite_note-zNyvS-186) Belgium subsequently has one of the oldest populations in the world, with an average age of 41.6 years.[[172]](#cite_note-r40EE-187)

### Migration

As of 2007[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Belgium&action=edit), nearly 92% of the population had Belgian citizenship,[[173]](#cite_note-HhLsk-188) and other European Union member citizens account for around 6%. The prevalent foreign nationals were Italian (171,918), French (125,061), Dutch (116,970), [Moroccan](/wiki/Morocco "Morocco") (80,579), [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_people "Portuguese people") (43,509), Spanish (42,765), [Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_people "Turkish people") (39,419) and German (37,621).[[174]](#cite_note-uWPjZ-189)[[175]](#cite_note-O7sup-190) In 2007, there were 1.38 million foreign-born residents in Belgium, corresponding to 12.9% of the total population. Of these, 685,000 (6.4%) were born outside the EU, and 695,000 (6.5%) were born in another EU Member State.[[176]](#cite_note-7g7jl-191)[[177]](#cite_note-f3GT2-192)

At the beginning of 2012, people of foreign background and their descendants were estimated to have formed around 25% of the total population i.e. 2.8 million *new Belgians*.[[178]](#cite_note-npdata.be-193) Of these new Belgians, 1,200,000 are of European ancestry and 1,350,000[[179]](#cite_note-npdata-194) are from non-Western countries (most of them from [Morocco](/wiki/Morocco "Morocco"), [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey"), and the [DR Congo](/wiki/Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Democratic Republic of the Congo")). Since the modification of the [Belgian nationality law](/wiki/Belgian_nationality_law "Belgian nationality law") in 1984, more than [1.3 million migrants have acquired Belgian citizenship](/wiki/Demographics_of_Belgium "Demographics of Belgium"). The largest group of immigrants and their descendants in Belgium are [Italian Belgians](/wiki/Italian_Belgians "Italian Belgians") and [Moroccan Belgians](/wiki/Moroccans_in_Belgium "Moroccans in Belgium").[[180]](#cite_note-195) 89.2% of inhabitants of [Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_people "Turkish people") origin have been naturalized, as have 88.4% of people of Moroccan background, 75.4% of Italians, 56.2% of the French and 47.8% of Dutch people.[[179]](#cite_note-npdata-194)

[Statbel](/wiki/Statistics_Belgium "Statistics Belgium") released figures of the Belgian population in relation to the origin of people in Belgium. According to the data, as of 1 January 2021, 67.3% of the Belgian population was of ethnic Belgian origin, and 32.7% were of foreign origin or nationality, with 20.3% of those of a foreign nationality or ethnic group originating from neighbouring countries. The study also found that 74.5% of the [Brussels-Capital Region](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") were of non-Belgian origin, of which 13.8% originated from neighbouring countries.[[181]](#cite_note-Demographics2020-196)
