### Languages

Main article: [Languages of Belgium](/wiki/Languages_of_Belgium "Languages of Belgium")

Estimated distribution of primary languages in Belgium

|  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | |  |  | |
| [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language") | |  | 59% | |
| [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") | |  | 40% | |
| [German](/wiki/German_language "German language") | |  | 1% | |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Brussels_signs.jpg/250px-Brussels_signs.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brussels_signs.jpg)

[Bilingual](/wiki/Bilingual "Bilingual") signs in Brussels

Belgium has three official languages: Dutch, French and German. A number of non-official minority languages are spoken as well.[[183]](#cite_note-Ethnologue-16thEd-198) As no census exists, there are no official statistical data regarding the distribution or usage of Belgium's three official languages or their [dialects](/wiki/Dialect "Dialect").[[184]](#cite_note-gkqeP-199) However, various criteria, including the language(s) of parents, of education, or the second-language status of foreign born, may provide suggested figures. An estimated 60% of the Belgian population are native speakers of Dutch (often referred to as [Flemish](/wiki/Flemish_people "Flemish people")), and 40% of the population speaks French natively. French-speaking Belgians are often referred to as Walloons, although the French speakers in Brussels are not Walloons.[[p]](#cite_note-200)

The total number of native Dutch speakers is estimated to be about 6.23 million, concentrated in the northern Flanders region, while native French speakers number 3.32 million in Wallonia and an estimated 870,000 (or 85%) in the officially bilingual Brussels-Capital Region.[[q]](#cite_note-201)[[185]](#cite_note-britishcouncil-202) The [German-speaking Community](/wiki/German-speaking_Community_of_Belgium "German-speaking Community of Belgium") is made up of 73,000 people in the east of the [Walloon Region](/wiki/Wallonia "Wallonia"); around 10,000 German and 60,000 Belgian nationals are speakers of German. Roughly 23,000 more German speakers live in municipalities near the official Community.[[186]](#cite_note-German-speaking_Community-203)[[187]](#cite_note-sTtZw-204)[[188]](#cite_note-UMYYg-205)[[189]](#cite_note-ofCHC-206)

Both [Belgian Dutch](/wiki/Belgian_Dutch "Belgian Dutch") and [Belgian French](/wiki/Belgian_French "Belgian French") have [minor differences](/wiki/Cognate "Cognate") in vocabulary and semantic nuances from the varieties spoken respectively in the Netherlands and France. Many Flemish people still speak [dialects of Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_dialects "Dutch dialects") in their local environment. [Walloon](/wiki/Walloon_language "Walloon language"), considered either as a dialect of French or a distinct [Romance language](/wiki/Romance_language "Romance language"),[[190]](#cite_note-yBPue-207)[[191]](#cite_note-S2M27-208) is now only understood and spoken occasionally, mostly by elderly people. Walloon is divided into four dialects, which along with those of [Picard](/wiki/Picard_language "Picard language"),[[192]](#cite_note-Ethnologue-15thEd-209) are rarely used in public life and have largely been replaced by French.

### Religion

Main article: [Religion in Belgium](/wiki/Religion_in_Belgium "Religion in Belgium")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Bruxelles_-_Basilique_Nationale_du_Sacr%C3%A9-C%C5%93ur_%C3%A0_Koekelberg_%285%29.jpg/250px-Bruxelles_-_Basilique_Nationale_du_Sacr%C3%A9-C%C5%93ur_%C3%A0_Koekelberg_%285%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bruxelles_-_Basilique_Nationale_du_Sacr%C3%A9-C%C5%93ur_%C3%A0_Koekelberg_(5).jpg)

[National Basilica of the Sacred Heart](/wiki/Basilica_of_the_Sacred_Heart,_Brussels "Basilica of the Sacred Heart, Brussels") in [Koekelberg](/wiki/Koekelberg "Koekelberg"), Brussels

The [Constitution of Belgium](/wiki/Constitution_of_Belgium "Constitution of Belgium") provides for freedom of religion, and the government respects this right in practice.[[193]](#cite_note-MarshallCavendish2009-210) Belgium officially recognizes three religions: Christianity (Catholic, Protestantism, Orthodox churches and Anglicanism), Islam and Judaism.[[194]](#cite_note-01gjU-211) During the reigns of [Albert I](/wiki/Albert_I_of_Belgium "Albert I of Belgium") and [Baudouin](/wiki/Baudouin_I_of_Belgium "Baudouin I of Belgium"), the [Belgian royal family](/wiki/Belgian_royal_family "Belgian royal family") had a reputation of deeply rooted Catholicism.[[193]](#cite_note-MarshallCavendish2009-210)

Catholicism has traditionally been Belgium's majority religion; being especially strong in Flanders. However, by 2009 Sunday [church attendance](/wiki/Church_attendance "Church attendance") was 5% for Belgium in total; 3% in Brussels,[[195]](#cite_note-oLN9X-212) and 5.4% in Flanders. Church attendance in 2009 in Belgium was roughly half of the Sunday church attendance in 1998 (11% for the total of Belgium in 1998).[[196]](#cite_note-DSDy4-213) Despite the drop in church attendance, Catholic identity nevertheless remains an important part of Belgium's culture.[[193]](#cite_note-MarshallCavendish2009-210)

The European Social Survey in 2023 found that people who did not belong to a religion comprised 59% of the population. The share of Christians was 34%, with Catholicism being the largest denomination at 31% of the population. Protestants and other Christians comprised 2% and Orthodox Christians comprised 1%. Islam is the second largest religion with 6% of the population.[[2]](#cite_note-ESS2023A-2)[[3]](#cite_note-ESS2023B-3)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/De_Hoofdsynagoog_van_Brussel_%281874-1878%29%2C_n.o.v._arch._D._de_Keyzer_-_interieur%2C_koor_-_355610_-_onroerenderfgoed.jpg/250px-De_Hoofdsynagoog_van_Brussel_%281874-1878%29%2C_n.o.v._arch._D._de_Keyzer_-_interieur%2C_koor_-_355610_-_onroerenderfgoed.jpg)](/wiki/File:De_Hoofdsynagoog_van_Brussel_(1874-1878),_n.o.v._arch._D._de_Keyzer_-_interieur,_koor_-_355610_-_onroerenderfgoed.jpg)

Interior of the [Great Synagogue of Brussels](/wiki/Great_Synagogue_of_Brussels "Great Synagogue of Brussels")

According to the Eurobarometer 2010,[[197]](#cite_note-Special_Eurobarometer_393-214) 37% of Belgian citizens believe in God, 31% in some sort of spirit or life-force. 27% do not believe in any sort of spirit, God, or life-force. 5% did not respond. According to the Eurobarometer 2021, 49% of the total population of Belgium adhered to [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"), with [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholicism "Catholicism") being the largest denomination with 44%. Protestants comprised 1% and Orthodox Christians were 1% of the total, other Christians were 3%. Non-religious people comprised 41% of the population and were divided between atheists (15%) and agnostics or non-believers (26%). A further 2% of the population was Muslim.[[198]](#cite_note-EB2021-215)

In the early 2000s, there were approximately 42,000 Jews in Belgium. The [Jewish Community of Antwerp](/wiki/Jewish_Community_of_Antwerp "Jewish Community of Antwerp") (numbering some 18,000) is one of the largest in Europe, and one of the last places in the world where [Yiddish](/wiki/Yiddish "Yiddish") is the primary language of a large Jewish community (mirroring certain Orthodox and Hasidic communities in New York, New Jersey, and Israel). In addition, most Jewish children in Antwerp receive a Jewish education.[[199]](#cite_note-2n62d-216) There are several Jewish newspapers and more than 45 active synagogues (30 of which are in Antwerp) in the country.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Great_Mosque_of_Brussels.jpg/250px-Great_Mosque_of_Brussels.jpg)](/wiki/File:Great_Mosque_of_Brussels.jpg)

The [Great Mosque of Brussels](/wiki/Great_Mosque_of_Brussels "Great Mosque of Brussels"), former seat of the Islamic and Cultural Centre of Belgium

Sociologist Jan Hertogen estimated that in 2022 approximately 9.6% of the Belgian population was [Muslim](/wiki/Islam_in_Belgium "Islam in Belgium"). Muslims constituted 24.1% of the population of [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"), 8% of [Wallonia](/wiki/Wallonia "Wallonia") and 7.6% of [Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders").[[200]](#cite_note-217)[[201]](#cite_note-218)

### Health

Main article: [Healthcare in Belgium](/wiki/Healthcare_in_Belgium "Healthcare in Belgium")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/UZA_ziekenhuis.jpg/250px-UZA_ziekenhuis.jpg)](/wiki/File:UZA_ziekenhuis.jpg)

University Hospital of Antwerp

The Belgians enjoy good health. According to 2012 estimates, the average life expectancy is 79.65 years.[[135]](#cite_note-CIA-149) Since 1960, life expectancy has, in line with the European average, grown by two months per year. Death in Belgium is mainly due to heart and vascular disorders, [neoplasms](/wiki/Neoplasm "Neoplasm"), disorders of the respiratory system and unnatural causes of death (accidents, suicide). Non-natural causes of death and cancer are the most common causes of death for females up to age 24 and males up to age 44.[[202]](#cite_note-Corens2007-219)

[Healthcare in Belgium](/wiki/Healthcare_in_Belgium "Healthcare in Belgium") is financed through both social security contributions and taxation. Health insurance is compulsory. Health care is delivered by a mixed public and private system of independent medical practitioners and public, university and semi-private hospitals. Health care service are payable by the patient and reimbursed later by health insurance institutions, but for ineligible categories (of patients and services) so-called 3rd party payment systems exist.[[202]](#cite_note-Corens2007-219) The Belgian health care system is supervised and financed by the federal government, the Flemish and Walloon Regional governments; and the German Community also has (indirect) oversight and responsibilities.[[202]](#cite_note-Corens2007-219)

For the first time in Belgian history, the first child was euthanized following the 2-year mark of the removal of the euthanization age restrictions. The child had been euthanized due to an incurable disease that was inflicted upon the child. Although there may have been some support for the euthanization there is a possibility of controversy due to the issue revolving around the subject of assisted suicide.[[203]](#cite_note-cnn-220)

Excluding assisted suicide, Belgium has the highest suicide rate in Western Europe and one of the [highest suicide rates](/wiki/Suicide_rate_by_country "Suicide rate by country") in the [developed world](/wiki/Developed_countries "Developed countries") (exceeded only by Lithuania, South Korea, and Latvia).[[204]](#cite_note-dVw5w-221)

### Education

Main article: [Education in Belgium](/wiki/Education_in_Belgium "Education in Belgium")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/2011-09-24_17.42_Leuven%2C_universiteitsbibliotheek_ceg74154_foto4.jpg/250px-2011-09-24_17.42_Leuven%2C_universiteitsbibliotheek_ceg74154_foto4.jpg)](/wiki/File:2011-09-24_17.42_Leuven,_universiteitsbibliotheek_ceg74154_foto4.jpg)

The Central Library of the [KU Leuven](/wiki/KU_Leuven "KU Leuven") University

Education is compulsory from 6 to 18 years of age for Belgians.[[205]](#cite_note-Hofman_Hofman_Gray_Daly-222) Among [OECD](/wiki/Organisation_for_Economic_Co-operation_and_Development "Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development") countries in 2002, Belgium had the third highest proportion of 18- to 21-year-olds enrolled in [postsecondary education](/wiki/Postsecondary_education "Postsecondary education"), at 42%.[[206]](#cite_note-NCES_Tbl-388-223) Though an estimated 99% of the adult population is literate, concern is rising over [functional illiteracy](/wiki/Functional_illiteracy "Functional illiteracy").[[192]](#cite_note-Ethnologue-15thEd-209)[[207]](#cite_note-UNDP-224) The [Programme for International Student Assessment](/wiki/Programme_for_International_Student_Assessment "Programme for International Student Assessment") (PISA), coordinated by the OECD, currently ranks Belgium's education as the 19th best in the world, being significantly higher than the OECD average.[[208]](#cite_note-OECD_PISA-2006-225) Education is organized separately by each community. The Flemish Community scores noticeably above the French and German-speaking Communities.[[209]](#cite_note-De-Meyer_Pauly_Van-de-Poele-226)

Mirroring the structure of the 19th-century Belgian political landscape, characterized by the [Liberal](/wiki/Liberal_Party_(Belgium) "Liberal Party (Belgium)") and the [Catholic parties](/wiki/Catholic_Party_(Belgium) "Catholic Party (Belgium)"), the educational system is segregated into secular and religious schools. The secular branch of schooling is controlled by the communities, the provinces, or the municipalities, while religious, mainly [Catholic branch](/wiki/Catholic_school "Catholic school") education, is organized by religious authorities, which are also subsidized and supervised by the communities.[[210]](#cite_note-De-Ley-227)

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of Belgium](/wiki/Culture_of_Belgium "Culture of Belgium")

Despite its [political and linguistic divisions](/wiki/Partition_of_Belgium "Partition of Belgium"), the region corresponding to today's Belgium has seen the flourishing of major artistic movements that have had tremendous influence on European art and culture. Nowadays, to a certain extent, cultural life is concentrated within each language Community, and a variety of barriers have made a shared cultural sphere less pronounced.[[19]](#cite_note-Fitzmaurice-25)[[211]](#cite_note-kED6Y-228)[[212]](#cite_note-PjUPH-229) Since the 1970s, there are no bilingual universities or colleges in the country except the [Royal Military Academy](/wiki/Royal_Military_Academy_(Belgium) "Royal Military Academy (Belgium)") and the [Antwerp Maritime Academy](/wiki/Antwerp_Maritime_Academy "Antwerp Maritime Academy").[[213]](#cite_note-S2dnp-230)

### Fine arts

See also: [List of Belgian painters](/wiki/List_of_Belgian_painters "List of Belgian painters"), [Architecture of Belgium](/wiki/Architecture_of_Belgium "Architecture of Belgium"), and [Music of Belgium](/wiki/Music_of_Belgium "Music of Belgium")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Lamgods_open.jpg/250px-Lamgods_open.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lamgods_open.jpg)

*The [Ghent Altarpiece](/wiki/Ghent_Altarpiece "Ghent Altarpiece"): The Adoration of the Mystic Lamb* (interior view), painted 1432 by [van Eyck](/wiki/Jan_van_Eyck "Jan van Eyck")

Contributions to painting and architecture have been especially rich. The [Mosan art](/wiki/Mosan_art "Mosan art"), the [Early Netherlandish](/wiki/Early_Netherlandish_painting "Early Netherlandish painting"),[[214]](#cite_note-Nv4HY-231) the [Flemish Renaissance](/wiki/Dutch_and_Flemish_Renaissance_painting "Dutch and Flemish Renaissance painting") and [Baroque painting](/wiki/Flemish_Baroque_painting "Flemish Baroque painting")[[215]](#cite_note-HFu1w-232) and major examples of [Romanesque](/wiki/Romanesque_architecture "Romanesque architecture"), [Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_architecture "Gothic architecture"), [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance_architecture "Renaissance architecture") and [Baroque architecture](/wiki/Baroque_architecture "Baroque architecture")[[216]](#cite_note-LFwFJ-233) are milestones in the history of art. While the 15th century's art in the Low Countries is dominated by the religious paintings of [Jan van Eyck](/wiki/Jan_van_Eyck "Jan van Eyck") and [Rogier van der Weyden](/wiki/Rogier_van_der_Weyden "Rogier van der Weyden"), the 16th century is characterized by a broader panel of styles such as [Peter Breughel](/wiki/Pieter_Brueghel_the_Elder "Pieter Brueghel the Elder")'s landscape paintings and [Lambert Lombard](/wiki/Lambert_Lombard "Lambert Lombard")'s representation of the antique.[[217]](#cite_note-LjaN3-234) Though the Baroque style of [Peter Paul Rubens](/wiki/Peter_Paul_Rubens "Peter Paul Rubens") and [Anthony van Dyck](/wiki/Anthony_van_Dyck "Anthony van Dyck") flourished in the early 17th century in the Southern Netherlands,[[218]](#cite_note-bGwvp-235) it gradually declined thereafter.[[219]](#cite_note-I0Pep-236)[[220]](#cite_note-9nPUy-237)

During the 19th and 20th centuries many original [romantic](/wiki/Romanticism "Romanticism"), [expressionist](/wiki/Expressionism "Expressionism") and [surrealist](/wiki/Surrealism "Surrealism") Belgian painters emerged, including [James Ensor](/wiki/James_Ensor "James Ensor") and other artists belonging to the [Les XX](/wiki/Les_XX "Les XX") group, [Constant Permeke](/wiki/Constant_Permeke "Constant Permeke"), [Paul Delvaux](/wiki/Paul_Delvaux "Paul Delvaux") and [René Magritte](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_Magritte "René Magritte"). The avant-garde [CoBrA movement](/wiki/COBRA_(avant-garde_movement) "COBRA (avant-garde movement)") appeared in the 1950s, while the sculptor [Panamarenko](/wiki/Panamarenko "Panamarenko") remains a remarkable figure in contemporary art.[[221]](#cite_note-2jzAd-238)[[222]](#cite_note-1BIrR-239) Multidisciplinary artists [Jan Fabre](/wiki/Jan_Fabre "Jan Fabre"), [Wim Delvoye](/wiki/Wim_Delvoye "Wim Delvoye") and the painter [Luc Tuymans](/wiki/Luc_Tuymans "Luc Tuymans") are other internationally renowned figures on the contemporary art scene.

Belgian contributions to architecture also continued into the 19th and 20th centuries, including the work of [Victor Horta](/wiki/Victor_Horta "Victor Horta") and [Henry van de Velde](/wiki/Henry_van_de_Velde "Henry van de Velde"), who were major initiators of the [Art Nouveau](/wiki/Art_Nouveau "Art Nouveau") style.[[223]](#cite_note-ibivW-240)[[224]](#cite_note-jIXeI-241)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/TV-uitzending_Domino_Jacques_Brel_tijdens_de_opname_in_Amsterdam%2C_Marcanti%2C_Bestanddeelnr_914-8399.jpg/250px-TV-uitzending_Domino_Jacques_Brel_tijdens_de_opname_in_Amsterdam%2C_Marcanti%2C_Bestanddeelnr_914-8399.jpg)](/wiki/File:TV-uitzending_Domino_Jacques_Brel_tijdens_de_opname_in_Amsterdam,_Marcanti,_Bestanddeelnr_914-8399.jpg)

[Jacques Brel](/wiki/Jacques_Brel "Jacques Brel")

The [vocal music](/wiki/Vocal_music "Vocal music") of the [Franco-Flemish School](/wiki/Franco-Flemish_School "Franco-Flemish School") developed in the southern part of the Low Countries and was an important contribution to Renaissance culture.[[225]](#cite_note-Y5k58-242) In the 19th and 20th centuries, there was an emergence of major violinists, such as [Henri Vieuxtemps](/wiki/Henri_Vieuxtemps "Henri Vieuxtemps"), [Eugène Ysaÿe](/wiki/Eug%C3%A8ne_Ysa%C3%BFe "Eugène Ysaÿe") and [Arthur Grumiaux](/wiki/Arthur_Grumiaux "Arthur Grumiaux"), while [Adolphe Sax](/wiki/Adolphe_Sax "Adolphe Sax") invented the saxophone in 1846. The composer [César Franck](/wiki/C%C3%A9sar_Franck "César Franck") was born in Liège in 1822. Contemporary [popular music](/wiki/Popular_music "Popular music") in Belgium is also of repute. Jazz musicians [Django Reinhardt](/wiki/Django_Reinhardt "Django Reinhardt") and [Toots Thielemans](/wiki/Toots_Thielemans "Toots Thielemans") and singer [Jacques Brel](/wiki/Jacques_Brel "Jacques Brel") have achieved global fame. Nowadays, singer [Stromae](/wiki/Stromae "Stromae") has been a musical revelation in Europe and beyond, having great success. In rock/pop music, [Telex](/wiki/Telex_(band) "Telex (band)"), [Front 242](/wiki/Front_242 "Front 242"), [K's Choice](/wiki/K%27s_Choice "K's Choice"), [Hooverphonic](/wiki/Hooverphonic "Hooverphonic"), [Zap Mama](/wiki/Zap_Mama "Zap Mama"), [Soulwax](/wiki/Soulwax "Soulwax") and [dEUS](/wiki/Deus_(band) "Deus (band)") are well known. In the heavy metal scene, bands like [Machiavel](/wiki/Machiavel_(band) "Machiavel (band)"), [Channel Zero](/wiki/Channel_Zero_(band) "Channel Zero (band)") and [Enthroned](/wiki/Enthroned "Enthroned") have a worldwide fan-base.[[226]](#cite_note-XSYvA-243)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/GeorgesSimenon-StudioHarcourt-1952.png/250px-GeorgesSimenon-StudioHarcourt-1952.png)](/wiki/File:GeorgesSimenon-StudioHarcourt-1952.png)

Belgian writer [Georges Simenon](/wiki/Georges_Simenon "Georges Simenon")

Belgium has produced several well-known [authors](/wiki/Belgian_literature "Belgian literature"), including the poets [Emile Verhaeren](/wiki/Emile_Verhaeren "Emile Verhaeren"), [Guido Gezelle](/wiki/Guido_Gezelle "Guido Gezelle"), [Robert Goffin](/wiki/Robert_Goffin "Robert Goffin") and novelists [Hendrik Conscience](/wiki/Hendrik_Conscience "Hendrik Conscience"), [Stijn Streuvels](/wiki/Stijn_Streuvels "Stijn Streuvels"), [Georges Simenon](/wiki/Georges_Simenon "Georges Simenon"), [Suzanne Lilar](/wiki/Suzanne_Lilar "Suzanne Lilar"), [Hugo Claus](/wiki/Hugo_Claus "Hugo Claus") and [Amélie Nothomb](/wiki/Am%C3%A9lie_Nothomb "Amélie Nothomb"). The poet and playwright [Maurice Maeterlinck](/wiki/Maurice_Maeterlinck "Maurice Maeterlinck") won the [Nobel Prize in Literature](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "Nobel Prize in Literature") in 1911. *[The Adventures of Tintin](/wiki/The_Adventures_of_Tintin "The Adventures of Tintin")* by [Hergé](/wiki/Herg%C3%A9 "Hergé") is the best known of [Franco-Belgian comics](/wiki/Franco-Belgian_comics "Franco-Belgian comics"), but many other major authors, including [Peyo](/wiki/Peyo "Peyo") (*[The Smurfs](/wiki/The_Smurfs "The Smurfs")*), [André Franquin](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Franquin "André Franquin") (*[Gaston Lagaffe](/wiki/Gaston_Lagaffe "Gaston Lagaffe")*), [Morris](/wiki/Morris_(comics) "Morris (comics)") (*[Lucky Luke](/wiki/Lucky_Luke "Lucky Luke")*), [Willy Vandersteen](/wiki/Willy_Vandersteen "Willy Vandersteen") brought the Belgian cartoon strip industry a worldwide fame.[[227]](#cite_note-PtwmV-244) Additionally, famous crime author [Agatha Christie](/wiki/Agatha_Christie "Agatha Christie") created the character [Hercule Poirot](/wiki/Hercule_Poirot "Hercule Poirot"), a Belgian detective, who has served as a [protagonist](/wiki/Protagonist "Protagonist") in a number of her acclaimed mystery novels.

[Belgian cinema](/wiki/Cinema_of_Belgium "Cinema of Belgium") has brought a number of mainly Flemish novels to life on-screen.[[r]](#cite_note-245) Belgian directors include [André Delvaux](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Delvaux "André Delvaux"), [Stijn Coninx](/wiki/Stijn_Coninx "Stijn Coninx"), [Luc](/wiki/Luc_Dardenne "Luc Dardenne") and [Jean-Pierre Dardenne](/wiki/Jean-Pierre_Dardenne "Jean-Pierre Dardenne"); well-known actors include [Jean-Claude Van Damme](/wiki/Jean-Claude_Van_Damme "Jean-Claude Van Damme"), [Jan Decleir](/wiki/Jan_Decleir "Jan Decleir") and [Marie Gillain](/wiki/Marie_Gillain "Marie Gillain"); and successful films include *[Bullhead](/wiki/Bullhead_(film) "Bullhead (film)"), [Man Bites Dog](/wiki/Man_Bites_Dog_(film) "Man Bites Dog (film)")* and *[The Alzheimer Affair](/wiki/The_Alzheimer_Affair "The Alzheimer Affair")*.[[228]](#cite_note-yH1O7-246)
Belgium is also home to a number of successful fashion designers [Category:Belgian fashion designers](/wiki/Category:Belgian_fashion_designers "Category:Belgian fashion designers").

### Folklore

Further information: [Folklore of Belgium](/wiki/Folklore_of_Belgium "Folklore of Belgium")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Binche_-_Les_Gilles.jpg/250px-Binche_-_Les_Gilles.jpg)](/wiki/File:Binche_-_Les_Gilles.jpg)

The [Gilles](/wiki/Gilles "Gilles") of [Binche](/wiki/Binche "Binche"), in costume, wearing wax masks

Folklore plays a major role in Belgium's cultural life; the country has a comparatively high number of processions, [cavalcades](/wiki/Cavalcade "Cavalcade"), parades, [ommegangs](/wiki/Ommegang "Ommegang"), ducasses,[[s]](#cite_note-247) [kermesses](/wiki/Kermesse_(festival) "Kermesse (festival)"), and other local festivals, nearly always with an originally religious or [mythological background](/wiki/Mythology_of_the_Low_Countries "Mythology of the Low Countries"). The three-day [Carnival of Binche](/wiki/Carnival_of_Binche "Carnival of Binche"), near [Mons](/wiki/Mons,_Belgium "Mons, Belgium"), with its famous [Gilles](/wiki/Gilles "Gilles") (men dressed in high, plumed hats and bright costumes) is held just before [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent") (the 40 days between [Ash Wednesday](/wiki/Ash_Wednesday "Ash Wednesday") and [Easter](/wiki/Easter "Easter")). Together with the 'Processional Giants and Dragons' of [Ath](/wiki/Ath "Ath"), Brussels, [Dendermonde](/wiki/Dendermonde "Dendermonde"), [Mechelen](/wiki/Mechelen "Mechelen") and Mons, it is recognized by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") as a [Masterpiece of the Oral and Intangible Heritage of Humanity](/wiki/Masterpiece_of_the_Oral_and_Intangible_Heritage_of_Humanity "Masterpiece of the Oral and Intangible Heritage of Humanity").[[229]](#cite_note-AyTJs-248)

Other examples are the three-day [Carnival of Aalst](/wiki/Carnival_of_Aalst "Carnival of Aalst") in February or March; the still very religious processions of [the Holy Blood](/wiki/Procession_of_the_Holy_Blood "Procession of the Holy Blood") taking place in [Bruges](/wiki/Bruges "Bruges") in May, the [Virga Jesse procession](/wiki/Virga_Jesse_Basilica "Virga Jesse Basilica") held every seven years in [Hasselt](/wiki/Hasselt "Hasselt"), the [annual procession of Hanswijk](/wiki/Basilica_of_Our_Lady_of_Hanswijk "Basilica of Our Lady of Hanswijk") in Mechelen, the 15 August festivities in [Liège](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge "Liège"), and the Walloon festival in [Namur](/wiki/Namur "Namur"). Originated in 1832 and revived in the 1960s, the [Gentse Feesten](/wiki/Gentse_Feesten "Gentse Feesten") (a music and theatre festival organized in Ghent around [Belgian National Day](/wiki/Belgian_National_Day "Belgian National Day"), on 21 July) have become a modern tradition. Several of these festivals include sporting competitions, such as [cycling](/wiki/Cycle_sport "Cycle sport"), and many fall under the category of kermesses.

A major non-official holiday is [Saint Nicholas Day](/wiki/Saint_Nicholas_Day "Saint Nicholas Day") (Dutch: *[Sinterklaas](/wiki/Sinterklaas "Sinterklaas")*, French: *la Saint-Nicolas*), a festivity for children, and in Liège, for students.[[230]](#cite_note-X3HNa-249) It takes place each year on 6 December and is a sort of early [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas"). On the evening of 5 December, before going to bed, children put their shoes by the hearth with water or wine and a carrot for Saint Nicholas' horse or [donkey](/wiki/Donkey "Donkey"). According to tradition, Saint Nicholas comes at night and travels down the chimney. He then takes the food and water or wine, leaves presents, goes back up, feeds his horse or donkey, and continues on his course. He also knows whether children have been good or bad. This holiday is especially loved by children in Belgium and the Netherlands. Dutch immigrants imported the tradition into the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), where Saint Nicholas is now known as [Santa Claus](/wiki/Santa_Claus "Santa Claus").

### Cuisine

Main article: [Belgian cuisine](/wiki/Belgian_cuisine "Belgian cuisine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Moules_Frites.jpg/250px-Moules_Frites.jpg)](/wiki/File:Moules_Frites.jpg)

*[Moules-frites](/wiki/Moules-frites "Moules-frites")* or *mosselen met friet* is a representative dish of Belgium.

Belgium is famous for [beer](/wiki/Beer "Beer"), [chocolate](/wiki/Chocolate "Chocolate"), [waffles](/wiki/Waffle "Waffle") and [French fries](/wiki/French_fries "French fries"). The national dishes are [steak and fries](/wiki/Steak_frites "Steak frites"), and [mussels with fries](/wiki/Moules-frites "Moules-frites").[[231]](#cite_note-7hiRd-250)[[232]](#cite_note-cwjud-251)[[233]](#cite_note-252) Many highly ranked Belgian restaurants can be found in the most influential restaurant guides, such as the [Michelin Guide](/wiki/Michelin_Guide "Michelin Guide").[[234]](#cite_note-EWmqW-253) One of the many beers with the high prestige is that of the [Trappist](/wiki/Trappists "Trappists") [monks](/wiki/Monk "Monk"). Technically, it is an [ale](/wiki/Ale "Ale") and traditionally each abbey's beer is served in its own glass (the forms, heights and widths are different). There are only eleven breweries (six of them are Belgian) that are allowed to brew [Trappist beer](/wiki/Trappist_beer "Trappist beer").

Although Belgian gastronomy is connected to French cuisine, some [recipes](/wiki/Recipe "Recipe") were reputedly invented there, such as French fries (despite the name, although their exact place of origin is uncertain), [Flemish Carbonade](/wiki/Flemish_stew "Flemish stew") (a beef stew with beer, mustard and [bay laurel](/wiki/Bay_laurel "Bay laurel")), [speculaas](/wiki/Speculaas "Speculaas") (or *speculoos* in French, a sort of cinnamon and ginger-flavoured [shortcrust](/wiki/Shortcrust_pastry "Shortcrust pastry") [biscuit](/wiki/Biscuit "Biscuit")), [Brussels waffles](/wiki/Brussels_waffle "Brussels waffle") (and their variant, [Liège waffles](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge_waffle "Liège waffle")), [waterzooi](/wiki/Waterzooi "Waterzooi") (a broth made with chicken or fish, cream and vegetables), [endive](/wiki/Endive "Endive") with [bechamel](/wiki/Bechamel "Bechamel") sauce, [Brussels sprouts](/wiki/Brussels_sprout "Brussels sprout"), [Belgian pralines](/wiki/Belgian_pralines "Belgian pralines") (Belgium has some of the most renowned [chocolate](/wiki/Chocolate "Chocolate") houses), *charcuterie* (deli meats) and *[Paling in 't groen](/wiki/Paling_in_%27t_groen "Paling in 't groen")* (river [eels](/wiki/Eel "Eel") in a sauce of green herbs).

Brands of Belgian chocolate and pralines, like [Côte d'Or](/wiki/C%C3%B4te_d%27Or_(brand) "Côte d'Or (brand)"), [Neuhaus](/wiki/Chocolatier_Neuhaus "Chocolatier Neuhaus"), [Leonidas](/wiki/Leonidas_(chocolate_maker) "Leonidas (chocolate maker)") and [Godiva](/wiki/Godiva_Chocolatier "Godiva Chocolatier") are famous, as well as independent producers such as Burie and Del Rey in Antwerp and Mary's in Brussels.[[235]](#cite_note-6teth-254) Belgium produces over [1100 varieties of beer](/wiki/Belgian_beer "Belgian beer").[[236]](#cite_note-BierbijbelNieuwsblad-255)[[237]](#cite_note-BierbijbelKW-256) The Trappist beer of the [Abbey of Westvleteren](/wiki/Westvleteren_Brewery "Westvleteren Brewery") has repeatedly been rated the world's best beer.[[238]](#cite_note-0BjgZ-257)[[239]](#cite_note-yTWd9-258)[[240]](#cite_note-IOJxz-259)

The biggest brewer in the world by volume is [Anheuser-Busch InBev](/wiki/Anheuser-Busch_InBev "Anheuser-Busch InBev"), based in [Leuven](/wiki/Leuven "Leuven").[[241]](#cite_note-EpBpi-260)

### Sports

Main article: [Sport in Belgium](/wiki/Sport_in_Belgium "Sport in Belgium")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Eddy_Merckx_Molteni_1973.jpg/250px-Eddy_Merckx_Molteni_1973.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eddy_Merckx_Molteni_1973.jpg)

[Eddy Merckx](/wiki/Eddy_Merckx "Eddy Merckx"), widely regarded as one of the greatest cyclists of all time

Since the 1970s, sports clubs and federations are organized separately within each [language community](/wiki/Communities,_regions_and_language_areas_of_Belgium "Communities, regions and language areas of Belgium").[[242]](#cite_note-ch0h8-261) The *Administration de l'Éducation Physique et du Sport* ([ADEPS](/wiki/ADEPS "ADEPS")) is responsible for recognising the various French-speaking sports federations and also runs three sports centres in the Brussels-Capital Region.[[243]](#cite_note-262) Its Dutch-speaking counterpart is *Sport Vlaanderen* (formerly called [BLOSO](/wiki/Bloso "Bloso")).[[244]](#cite_note-263)

[Association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") is the most popular sport in both parts of Belgium; also very popular are cycling, tennis, swimming, judo[[245]](#cite_note-tR60q-264) and basketball.[[246]](#cite_note-DM5hq-265) The [Belgium national football team](/wiki/Belgium_national_football_team "Belgium national football team") has been among the best on the [FIFA World Rankings](/wiki/FIFA_World_Rankings "FIFA World Rankings") ever since November 2015, when it reached the top spot for the first time.[[247]](#cite_note-Fmads-266) Since the 1990s, the team has been the world's number one for the most years in history, only behind the records of [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil_National_Football_Team "Brazil National Football Team") and [Spain](/wiki/Spain_National_Football_Team "Spain National Football Team").[[248]](#cite_note-267) The team's golden generations with the world class players in the squad, namely [Eden Hazard](/wiki/Eden_Hazard "Eden Hazard"), [Kevin De Bruyne](/wiki/Kevin_De_Bruyne "Kevin De Bruyne"), [Jean-Marie Pfaff](/wiki/Jean-Marie_Pfaff "Jean-Marie Pfaff"), [Jan Ceulemans](/wiki/Jan_Ceulemans "Jan Ceulemans") achieved the bronze medals at [World Cup 2018](/wiki/2018_FIFA_World_Cup "2018 FIFA World Cup"), and silver medals at [Euro 1980](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1980 "UEFA Euro 1980"). Belgium hosted the [Euro 1972](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1972 "UEFA Euro 1972"), and co-hosted the [Euro 2000](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2000 "UEFA Euro 2000") with the Netherlands.

Belgians hold [the most Tour de France victories](/wiki/List_of_Tour_de_France_winners "List of Tour de France winners") of any country except France. They also have the most victories on the [UCI Road World Championships](/wiki/UCI_Road_World_Championships "UCI Road World Championships"). With five victories in the [Tour de France](/wiki/Tour_de_France "Tour de France") and numerous other cycling records, Belgian cyclist [Eddy Merckx](/wiki/Eddy_Merckx "Eddy Merckx") is often regarded as one of the greatest cyclists of all time.[[249]](#cite_note-rFgQf-268) [Philippe Gilbert](/wiki/Philippe_Gilbert "Philippe Gilbert") and [Remco Evenepoel](/wiki/Remco_Evenepoel "Remco Evenepoel") were the 2012 and 2022 world champions, respectively. Other well-known Belgian cyclists are [Tom Boonen](/wiki/Tom_Boonen "Tom Boonen"), [Wout van Aert](/wiki/Wout_van_Aert "Wout van Aert") and [Lotte Kopecky](/wiki/Lotte_Kopecky "Lotte Kopecky").

[Kim Clijsters](/wiki/Kim_Clijsters "Kim Clijsters") and [Justine Henin](/wiki/Justine_Henin "Justine Henin") both were [Player of the Year](/wiki/WTA_Awards "WTA Awards") in the [Women's Tennis Association](/wiki/Women%27s_Tennis_Association "Women's Tennis Association") as they were ranked the number one female tennis player.
The [Spa-Francorchamps](/wiki/Circuit_de_Spa-Francorchamps "Circuit de Spa-Francorchamps") motor-racing circuit hosts the [Formula One World Championship](/wiki/Formula_One_World_Championship "Formula One World Championship") [Belgian Grand Prix](/wiki/Belgian_Grand_Prix "Belgian Grand Prix"). The Belgian driver, [Jacky Ickx](/wiki/Jacky_Ickx "Jacky Ickx"), won eight Grands Prix and six [24 Hours of Le Mans](/wiki/24_Hours_of_Le_Mans "24 Hours of Le Mans") and finished twice as runner-up in the Formula One World Championship. Belgium also has a strong reputation in, [motocross](/wiki/Motocross "Motocross") with the riders [Joël Robert](/wiki/Jo%C3%ABl_Robert "Joël Robert"), [Roger De Coster](/wiki/Roger_De_Coster "Roger De Coster"), [Georges Jobé](/wiki/Georges_Job%C3%A9 "Georges Jobé"), [Eric Geboers](/wiki/Eric_Geboers "Eric Geboers") and [Stefan Everts](/wiki/Stefan_Everts "Stefan Everts"), among others.[[250]](#cite_note-rCTsM-269)

Sporting events annually held in Belgium include the [Memorial Van Damme](/wiki/Memorial_Van_Damme "Memorial Van Damme") athletics competition, the [Belgian Grand Prix](/wiki/Belgian_Grand_Prix "Belgian Grand Prix") Formula One, and a number of [classic cycle races](/wiki/Classic_cycle_races "Classic cycle races") such as the [Tour of Flanders](/wiki/Tour_of_Flanders_(disambiguation) "Tour of Flanders (disambiguation)") and [Liège–Bastogne–Liège](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge%E2%80%93Bastogne%E2%80%93Li%C3%A8ge "Liège–Bastogne–Liège"). The [1920 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1920_Summer_Olympics "1920 Summer Olympics") were held in Antwerp. The [1977 European Basketball Championship](/wiki/1977_European_Basketball_Championship "1977 European Basketball Championship") was held in [Liège](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge "Liège") and [Ostend](/wiki/Ostend "Ostend").

7. **[^](#cite_ref-30)** Between 1885 and 1908, the [Congo Free State](/wiki/Congo_Free_State "Congo Free State"), which was privately owned by King [Leopold II of Belgium](/wiki/Leopold_II_of_Belgium "Leopold II of Belgium"), was characterized by [widespread atrocities](/wiki/Atrocities_in_the_Congo_Free_State "Atrocities in the Congo Free State") and disease; amid public outcry in Europe, Belgium annexed the territory as a colony.[[23]](#cite_note-29)
8. **[^](#cite_ref-39)** The Morini were located along the coast of modern Belgium, particularly in the [Pas-de-Calais](/wiki/Pas-de-Calais "Pas-de-Calais") region, centered around [Boulogne-sur-Mer](/wiki/Boulogne-sur-Mer "Boulogne-sur-Mer"). Their territory extended from the [River Canache](/wiki/Canche "Canche") to the [River Scheldt](/wiki/River_Scheldt "River Scheldt").[[31]](#cite_note-38)
9. **[^](#cite_ref-41)** The Menapii dwelt near the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea"), around present-day [Cassel](/wiki/Cassel,_Nord "Cassel, Nord"), in the region now part of Belgium.[[32]](#cite_note-40)
10. **[^](#cite_ref-43)** The Nervii inhabited the central part of modern Belgium, including Brussels, extending southwards to [Cambrai](/wiki/Cambrai "Cambrai") in [French Hainaut](/wiki/French_Hainaut "French Hainaut"). Their territory included areas along the [Haine](/wiki/Haine "Haine") and [Sambre](/wiki/Sambre "Sambre") rivers.[[33]](#cite_note-42)
11. **[^](#cite_ref-45)** The Germani Cisrhenani inhabited regions on the eastern side of the [Rhine](/wiki/Rhine "Rhine"), corresponding to parts of modern-day Germany, but their influence extended into the northeastern part of Gaul.[[34]](#cite_note-gca-44)
12. **[^](#cite_ref-46)** The [Aduatuci](/wiki/Aduatuci "Aduatuci") lived in the eastern part of modern-day Belgium, particularly in the [Meuse valley](/wiki/Meuse "Meuse") and the [Hesbaye region](/wiki/Hesbaye "Hesbaye").[[34]](#cite_note-gca-44)
13. **[^](#cite_ref-128)** Since 2011, the French Community has used the name "Wallonia-Brussels Federation" (French: *Fédération Wallonie-Bruxelles*), which is controversial because its name in the [Belgian Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Belgium "Constitution of Belgium") has not changed and because it is seen as a political statement.[[114]](#cite_note-126)[[115]](#cite_note-127)
14. **[^](#cite_ref-131)** The Constitution set out seven institutions each of which can have a parliament, government and administration. In fact, there are only six such bodies because the Flemish Region merged into the Flemish Community. This single Flemish body thus exercises powers about Community matters in the bilingual area of Brussels-Capital and in the Dutch language area, while about Regional matters only in Flanders.
15. **[^](#cite_ref-151)** The richest (per capita income) of Belgium's three regions is the [Flemish Region](/wiki/Flemish_Region "Flemish Region"), followed by the [Walloon Region](/wiki/Walloon_Region "Walloon Region") and lastly the [Brussels-Capital Region](/wiki/Brussels-Capital_Region "Brussels-Capital Region"). 16. **[^](#cite_ref-200)** Native speakers of Dutch living in Wallonia and of French in Flanders are relatively small minorities that furthermore largely balance one another, hence attributing all inhabitants of each unilingual area to the area's language can cause only insignificant inaccuracies (99% can speak the language). Dutch: Flanders' 6.079 million inhabitants and about 15% of Brussels' 1.019 million are 6.23 million or 59.3% of the 10.511 million inhabitants of Belgium (2006); German: 70,400 in the German-speaking Community (which has [language facilities](/wiki/Municipalities_with_language_facilities "Municipalities with language facilities") for its less than 5% French-speakers) and an estimated 20,000–25,000 speakers of German in the Walloon Region outside the geographical boundaries of their official Community, or 0.9%; French: in the latter area as well as mainly in the rest of Wallonia (3.321 million) and 85% of the Brussels inhabitants (0.866 million) thus 4.187 million or 39.8%; together indeed 100%.
17. **[^](#cite_ref-201)** Flemish Academic Eric Corijn (initiator of [Charta 91](http://www.charta91.be/)), at a colloquium regarding Brussels, on 2001-12-05, states that in Brussels 91% of the population speaks French at home, either alone or with another language, and about 20% speaks Dutch at home, either alone (9%) or with French (11%)—After ponderation, the repartition can be estimated at between 85 and 90% French-speaking, and the remaining are Dutch-speaking, corresponding to the estimations based on languages chosen in Brussels by citizens for their official documents (ID, driving licenses, weddings, birth, sex, and so on); all these statistics on language are also available at Belgian Department of Justice (for weddings, birth, sex), Department of Transport (for Driving licenses), Department of Interior (for IDs), because there are no means to know *precisely* the proportions since Belgium has abolished 'official' linguistic censuses, thus official documents on language choices can only be estimations. For a web source on this topic, see e.g. [General online sources: Janssens, Rudi](#General)
18. **[^](#cite_ref-245)** Notable Belgian films based on works by Flemish authors include: *De Witte* (author [Ernest Claes](/wiki/Ernest_Claes "Ernest Claes")) movie by Jan Vanderheyden and Edith Kiel in 1934, remake as *De Witte van Sichem* directed by [Robbe De Hert](/wiki/Robbe_De_Hert "Robbe De Hert") in 1980; *De man die zijn haar kort liet knippen* ([Johan Daisne](/wiki/Johan_Daisne "Johan Daisne")) [André Delvaux](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Delvaux "André Delvaux") 1965; *Mira* ('De teleurgang van de Waterhoek' by [Stijn Streuvels](/wiki/Stijn_Streuvels "Stijn Streuvels")) [Fons Rademakers](/wiki/Fons_Rademakers "Fons Rademakers") 1971; *[Malpertuis](/wiki/Malpertuis "Malpertuis") (aka The Legend of Doom House)* ([Jean Ray](/wiki/Jean_Ray_(author) "Jean Ray (author)") [pen name of Flemish author who mainly wrote in French, or as John Flanders in Dutch]) [Harry Kümel](/wiki/Harry_K%C3%BCmel "Harry Kümel") 1971; *De loteling* ([Hendrik Conscience](/wiki/Hendrik_Conscience "Hendrik Conscience")) Roland Verhavert 1974; *Dood van een non* ([Maria Rosseels](/wiki/Maria_Rosseels "Maria Rosseels")) Paul Collet and Pierre Drouot 1975; *Pallieter* ([Felix Timmermans](/wiki/Felix_Timmermans "Felix Timmermans")) Roland Verhavert 1976; *De komst van Joachim Stiller* ([Hubert Lampo](/wiki/Hubert_Lampo "Hubert Lampo")) [Harry Kümel](/wiki/Harry_K%C3%BCmel "Harry Kümel") 1976; *[De Leeuw van Vlaanderen](/wiki/De_Leeuw_van_Vlaanderen_(novel) "De Leeuw van Vlaanderen (novel)")* ([Hendrik Conscience](/wiki/Hendrik_Conscience "Hendrik Conscience")) [Hugo Claus](/wiki/Hugo_Claus "Hugo Claus") (a famous author himself) 1985; *[Daens](/wiki/Daens_(film) "Daens (film)")* ('Pieter Daens' by [Louis Paul Boon](/wiki/Louis_Paul_Boon "Louis Paul Boon")) [Stijn Coninx](/wiki/Stijn_Coninx "Stijn Coninx") 1992; see also [Filmarchief *les DVD!s de la cinémathèque*](https://web.archive.org/web/20070514023803/http://www.ledoux.be/nl/dvdmain.htm) (in Dutch). Retrieved on 7 June 2007.
19. **[^](#cite_ref-247)** The Dutch word *ommegang* is here used in the sense of an entirely or mainly non-religious procession, or the non-religious part thereof—see also [its article on the Dutch-language Wikipedia](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ommegang "nl:Ommegang"); the Processional Giants of Brussels, Dendermonde and Mechelen mentioned in this paragraph are part of each city's *ommegang*. The French word *ducasse* refers also to a procession; the mentioned Processional Giants of Ath and Mons are part of each city's *ducasse*.

[ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-3-87118-597-7](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-3-87118-597-7 "Special:BookSources/978-3-87118-597-7"). "Zur prestige Sprache wurde in den Spanischen Niederlanden ganz eindeutig das Französische. Die Vertreter Spaniens beherrschten normalerweise das Französische, nicht aber das Niederländische; ein beachtlicher Teil der am Hofe tätigen Adligen stammte aus Wallonien, das sich ja eher auf die spanische Seite geschlagen hatte als Flandern und Brabant. In dieser Situation war es selbstverständlich, dass die flämischen Adligen, die im Laufe der Zeit immer mehr ebenfalls zu Hofbeamten wurden, sich des Französischen bedienen mussten, wenn sie als gleichwertig anerkannt werden wollten. [Transl.: The prestigious language in the Spanish Netherlands was clearly French. Spain's representatives usually mastered French but not Dutch; a notable part of the nobles at the court came from Wallonia, which had taken party for the Spanish side to a higher extent than Flanders and Brabant. It was therefore evident within this context that the Flemish nobility, of which a progressively larger number became servants of the court, had to use French, if it wanted to get acknowledged as well.]"

"het Cruijdeboeck, dat in 1554 verscheen. Dit meesterwerk was na de bijbel in die tijd het meest vertaalde boek. This masterpiece was, after the Bible, the most translated book in that time. It continued to be republished for more than a century and for more than two centuries it was the mostly used referential about herbs. It is a work with world fame and great scientific value. The new thoughts written down by Dodoens, became the building bricks for botanists and physicians of later generations.)*"
